title: Pro tier = literally unlimited unmetered Anthropic spend, and Pro is free to mint
mechanism: checkAndConsume() short-circuits `if (unlimited) return {allowed:true, remaining:null}` for any tier != free, so Pro/trial devices are NEVER counted against any quota. The ONLY server-side spend guard is the free-tier 15-drafts/week counter; Pro drafts (interactive suggest, autodraft, threadIntel, insight, dossier, ask, voicePersona — all 7 sites share this one wire) have zero server ceiling. There is no global Anthropic budget or circuit-breaker anywhere. And 'Pro' is trivially forgeable: validateLicenseKey() returns valid Pro for ANY key starting 'OSMO-', which flips subscriptionActive=true → resolveTier returns 'pro' → unlimited.
breaksAt: Immediately for a single motivated actor; also organically — a legit heavy Pro user driving per-thread threadIntel + 30/day autodraft + ad-hoc ask/dossier can run the Anthropic bill unbounded with no server backstop.
reasoning: Every link verified in code. (1) Device tokens are minted with zero auth at web/app/api/device/register/route.ts:9-19. (2) web/app/api/license/validate/route.ts:16-19 accepts any key via validateLicenseKey, which returns valid Pro for any 'OSMO-' prefix (entitlement.ts:45-48), durably setting subscriptionActive=true (Supabase when live). (3) resolveTier returns 'pro' on subscriptionActive (entitlement.ts:19). (4) In suggest/route.ts:67-69, checkAndConsume(..., tier!=='free') hits quota.ts:29 'if (unlimited) return {allowed:true, remaining:null}' so Pro/trial are never counted; the only counter is the free 15/week bucket. (5) Grep confirms /api/suggest is the single Anthropic egress (only file touching api.anthropic.com/ANTHROPIC_API_KEY) and there is NO global budget or circuit-breaker anywhere; the sole rate limit in the codebase is a per-device window on the unrelated /api/enrich/person. max_tokens:700 caps one response, not aggregate volume. So an anonymous actor can register a device, POST a fake OSMO- key to become durable Pro, and loop /api/suggest against a real Anthropic key with no per-request or aggregate ceiling; legitimate heavy Pro use is equally uncapped. Mitigating nuance: the OSMO- validator is an explicit pre-Stripe mock and per-user unlimited Pro is intentional, but the absence of any aggregate Anthropic spend cap is a durable gap that persists after the mock is replaced, keeping this critical for a single-instance solo-margin app that holds the key in production. Plan Phase 0 elaboration covers only the systemCore/Safety/auth MUST-FIX; no phase adds a spend budget, meters Pro, or closes the forgeable-license path, and the planned auth fix would not address either half.
refined: The proxy has no aggregate/global Anthropic spend cap or circuit-breaker and exempts all Pro/trial devices from any server counter (quota.ts:29), while Pro is anonymously mintable (open device registration plus a mock validateLicenseKey granting Pro for any 'OSMO-' key at entitlement.ts:47 sets subscriptionActive=true then resolveTier=pro), so a production Anthropic key can be driven to unbounded cost by any anonymous actor and by legitimate heavy Pro use alike.

title: Client-chosen `model` forwarded to Anthropic verbatim — force the most expensive model per call
mechanism: The suggest route reads `body.model` (default 'claude-sonnet-5') and passes it straight into the Anthropic request body with no allowlist/validation. A caller can substitute the most expensive available model (e.g. an Opus tier) and multiply per-request cost several-fold on EVERY call. Combined with the missing global budget and the unauthed path below, this turns each burned request into a maximally expensive one.
breaksAt: Every request — the multiplier applies immediately and stacks on top of any volume-based runaway.
evidence: web/app/api/suggest/route.ts:46 (model = body.model ?? default), :79 (model passed to anthropicBody unvalidated)
reasoning: Code confirms the mechanism: web/app/api/suggest/route.ts:46 reads `body.model ?? "claude-sonnet-5"` and :79 forwards it verbatim into anthropicBody sent to Anthropic (:88-96). Grep of web/ shows `model` appears only in this route (:20/:46/:79/:128) with no allowlist, enum, or config-driven whitelist anywhere — so a raw HTTP caller can set `model` to an expensive Opus-tier id and it is honored. Not mitigated. However, severity "high" overstates the standalone impact: (1) with no ANTHROPIC_API_KEY the route returns a zero-cost mock (:52-55), so it only bites in production; (2) output is capped at max_tokens 700 (:80), so the effect is a bounded per-token price multiplier (~5x for Opus) rather than an unbounded blowup; (3) free devices are still weekly-metered by checkAndConsume (:63-76). Its real financial damage is entirely conditional on the companion findings (unbounded client systemCore, missing global budget, keyless/no-auth passthrough) — it multiplies those, so it is a medium force-multiplier, not an independent high. Plan: Phase 0's elaborated fix covers only systemCore/Safety/auth; server-owned model selection is only implied by the Phase 2 signed config registry ("prompts/models iterate without OTA"), so partial.
refined: Any caller can set `model` in the POST body and it is forwarded to Anthropic with no allowlist (route.ts:46,79), letting a request pick a more expensive model — a real but bounded (~5x, output-capped) per-call cost multiplier whose damage depends on the separate no-auth/no-budget findings.

title: With ANTHROPIC_API_KEY set but OSMO_REQUIRE_AUTH!=1, anyone burns the key uncapped and unmetered
mechanism: Auth is only enforced when OSMO_REQUIRE_AUTH==='1'. Otherwise the route skips the 401 gate entirely. Quota is only applied when a device is resolvable from the bearer (`device = bearer ? deviceByToken : null`); with no/garbage bearer, device is null, so checkAndConsume is NEVER called — the request goes straight to Anthropic with a real key, unmetered. So the single config slip of not setting OSMO_REQUIRE_AUTH turns /api/suggest into an open, uncapped Anthropic relay for the whole internet.
breaksAt: As soon as the key is added without the auth flag — the confirmed default posture is 'keyless dev, auth optional', so this is the natural first production state.
evidence: web/app/api/suggest/route.ts:31-36 (auth only if OSMO_REQUIRE_AUTH==1), :61-63 (device null when no valid bearer => quota block skipped), :88-96 (real Anthropic call)
reasoning: Code confirms the mechanism precisely. web/app/api/suggest/route.ts:31-36 runs the 401 gate ONLY inside `if (process.env.OSMO_REQUIRE_AUTH === "1")`; with the flag unset/!=1 there is no auth check. route.ts:51-55 shows a set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY skips the mock and reaches the live call. route.ts:61-63 computes `device = bearer ? deviceByToken(bearer) : null`, and the ENTIRE quota block (checkAndConsume) is gated behind `if (device)` (line 63), so a request with no/garbage bearer yields device=null and quota is never consumed — straight to the unmetered Anthropic call at route.ts:88-96. No global mitigation exists: find shows no middleware.ts anywhere in /Users/home/Osmo, and OSMO_REQUIRE_AUTH is referenced only in this route — nothing couples 'key present' to 'auth required'. It is a fail-open design: protection is opt-in via a separate env flag, decoupled from the key it guards, and metering itself is gated on attacker-controllable device resolvability. The .env.local header ('runs fully in keyless DEMO mode with only the Anthropic key set') confirms the default posture. Severity is critical: unbounded, unattributed billing on the shared key plus an open Claude relay for the internet, trivially triggered by any POST with systemCore+userTurn. Not mitigated: the only guard is setting OSMO_REQUIRE_AUTH=1, which is exactly the missing config. planStatus partial: Phase 0 lists the auth MUST-FIX ('keyless any bearer passes') so it is not unaddressed, but the elaboration does not explicitly close the two sub-holes here — the env-flag fail-open coupling and the quota bypass when device is null (quota gated on `if(device)` rather than mandatory).
refined: When ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is set but OSMO_REQUIRE_AUTH!=1, /api/suggest skips the auth gate (route.ts:31-36) and, because the quota block is gated on `if (device)` with device=null for any missing/garbage bearer (route.ts:61-63), makes an uncapped, unmetered live Anthropic call (route.ts:88-96) — an open, internet-facing key-burn relay whose only guard is an opt-in env flag decoupled from the key.

title: NEW plan's background quality gate is a 2–4x LLM multiplier with no stated budget
mechanism: The proposed S4→S5 background path turns one draft into generate-2 (2 full generations) + a Haiku judge call + a conditional S5 repair generation = up to ~4 LLM calls per delivered draft. Layered on autodraft firing per inbound (up to 30/thread-day) and threadIntel per thread per inbound, the background tier multiplies the exact volume that today has no aggregate ceiling. The plan gates it to 'background only' and makes repair conditional, but specifies no global spend cap or per-account background budget — so the multiplier rides on top of the same uncapped Pro wire.
breaksAt: At the scale the plan is designed for — routine inbound volume for an active Pro user — background spend is 2–4x the already-uncapped foreground spend.
evidence: PLAN S4/S5 (generate-2 -> Haiku judge -> conditional repair); today's single-call wire it multiplies: App/Sources/App/AppModel.swift:844-878 (autodraft per inbound), :1110-1128 (threadIntel per thread)
reasoning: Mechanism is real. The plan specifies S4 generate-2, Haiku judge, S5 conditional repair as background pre-compute, and no phase states a per-account background spend budget (Phase 4 is background quality gate plus cold-start/outage; Phase 6 evidence-gated ceiling is deferred and about model quality, not spend). The uncapped Pro wire is verified: web/lib/license/quota.ts:29 returns unlimited for any non-free tier, so server quota guards only free tier (15/week); Entitlements.swift:10 confirms pro is unlimited. So layering a background multiplier on a server-uncapped tier with no budget is a genuine margin-planning gap. But framing and severity are off on three counts. First, the claim up-to-30-per-thread-day misreads the code: autodraftCap at AppModel.swift:827 is a single global per-device state and AutodraftPolicy.dailyCap=30 is a device-wide daily cap, a tighter de-facto ceiling bounding legit-user backgrounded volume to about 30/day. Second, 2-4x LLM calls conflates call count with cost: the judge is Haiku (much cheaper than a Sonnet generation) and repair is conditional, so the true cost multiplier is dominated by the second generation (about 2x), not 4x. Third, this is a plan-level budget omission on a proposed unbuilt tier, background and deferrable, and the underlying uncapped-Pro exposure is a pre-existing condition (a separate finding), not introduced by this gate, so high overstates it. Existing code does not mitigate: the 30/day autodraft cap is device-local and client-defeatable, not a server spend budget, and threadIntel (ensureIntel at AppModel.swift:1110) has no daily cap. planStatus partial is fair: background-only plus conditional-repair constrain the multiplier shape but no budget exists.
refined: Real but medium: the proposed Phase-4 background quality gate adds a background LLM multiplier of roughly 2x cost (not a flat 4x, since the judge is cheap Haiku and repair is conditional) on top of a Pro tier that is verifiably server-uncapped at web/lib/license/quota.ts:29, yet the plan states no per-account background budget; the exposure is bounded for legit users by a global 30-autodrafts-per-device-per-day cap (AutodraftPolicy.dailyCap, not 30 per thread as claimed), making this a margin-planning gap the plan should close rather than a high-severity flaw.

title: Person enrichment has no result cache; 2 paid upstreams guarded only by a per-process, per-device window
mechanism: enrichPerson() calls Unipile getUserProfile AND Exa searchPerson (numResults 5) on every request with NO result cache — re-enriching the same person re-hits both paid APIs. The only guard is a module-level Map of 10 requests/60s KEYED PER DEVICE. Because /api/device/register is open and unlimited, one actor mints N devices to get N×(10/60s) enrichment budget. The map is in-memory on one process, so a redeploy resets it, and it provides zero cross-request dedup for identical names. Worse, exa.ts retries 3x with backoff on 429/5xx, so a flaky/rate-limited Exa turns each admitted request into up to 3 paid Exa calls.
breaksAt: Sustained browsing or any multi-device/scripted actor; the per-device 10/60s cap is not a spend cap and is defeated by open device minting.
evidence: web/lib/enrich/person.ts:25-59 (no cache, Unipile+Exa each call); web/app/api/enrich/person/route.ts:12-19,38 (per-device per-process window); web/lib/enrich/exa.ts:18-35 (3x retry)
reasoning: Core mechanism holds. person.ts:25-71 has no server-side result cache: every admitted call re-hits Exa (searchPerson, numResults 5) and, when the device has a connected LinkedIn account + handle, Unipile getUserProfile. route.ts:12-22,38 shows the ONLY guard is a per-process, per-device module-level sliding window (10/60s) with no quota/entitlement/spend cap — contrast /api/suggest/route.ts:69 which calls checkAndConsume. device/register/route.ts:9-19 is a bare unauthenticated POST that always mints a device, so an actor can mint N devices for N×(10/60s) of Exa budget; the window is in-memory (redeploy resets, zero cross-request dedup for identical names). exa.ts:18-33 confirms 3x retry on 429/5xx. So this is a genuine third-party wallet-drain / cost-amplification gap. Two framing corrections drop it from high to medium: (1) The device-minting attack amplifies only Exa, NOT Unipile — freshly minted devices have no connected LinkedIn account (person.ts:38-40 requires status==='connected'), so accountId is undefined and the Unipile call is skipped; both upstreams fire together only for a single legitimately-connected device. (2) The '3x paid Exa calls' framing overstates cost — 429/5xx responses are throttle/error, not billed successful searches, so retries mainly add load, not guaranteed spend. Practical impact is bounded per device (Exa search+800-char content is comparatively cheap), the most expensive path (Anthropic LLM) is separately quota-gated, and the app caches enrichment locally (OsmoStore+Shell.swift:181-197) so normal usage doesn't spam the server. The real gap is the absence of any global/entitlement spend cap on enrichment plus open unlimited device registration. Not already mitigated server-side: the per-device window is the very guard the claim accounts for, and no server result cache or global budget exists. planStatus partial is correct: plan Phase 5 explicitly names an 'enrichment cache' (addresses the dedup half), but Phase 0's elaboration is scoped only to systemCore/Safety/auth and no phase adds a global enrichment spend cap or device-register rate limit.
refined: /api/enrich/person fans out to Exa (and, only for LinkedIn-connected devices, Unipile) with no server-side result cache and no global/entitlement spend cap — just a per-process, per-device 10/60s window — so an actor exploiting the open, unlimited /api/device/register can mint devices to multiply uncapped Exa spend (Unipile is not amplifiable this way because it requires a per-device connected account).

title: /api/connect/rebackfill has no rate limit and no in-flight guard — repeated/concurrent runs re-page paid Unipile history
mechanism: rebackfill sets the connection to 'backfilling' then fires backfillConnection fire-and-forget with no rate limit and no dedup on (deviceId,accountId). Each run pages up to MAX_MESSAGE_PAGES=200 message pages (250/page) plus listChats plus up to 60 listChatAttendees calls — all paid Unipile calls. The only stop is the loop bailing when status != 'backfilling', which does NOT prevent two concurrent rebackfills (both set 'backfilling' and both loop) or a rebackfill overlapping the initial notify-triggered backfill. Ingest dedup prevents duplicate ROWS but does nothing to prevent duplicate paid FETCHES.
breaksAt: Any scripted loop over the endpoint; each call ≈ up to ~260 paid Unipile requests, unbounded in aggregate.
evidence: web/app/api/connect/rebackfill/route.ts:22-27 (no rate limit, void backfill); web/lib/connections/backfill.ts:18 (MAX_MESSAGE_PAGES 200), :77-81 (paging loop / status-only bail), :64 (listChatAttendees), :59 (MAX_ATTENDEE_FETCHES 60)
reasoning: The claim's core mechanism misreads the code. rebackfill/route.ts:22-27 does NOT set the store connection status to 'backfilling' — it only publish()es an SSE doorbell (events.ts:26 has no store side effect); setConnectionStatus (memoryStore.ts:198) is never called. The message-paging loop guard at backfill.ts:80 reads the STORE status: `if (store.connectionById(accountId)?.status !== "backfilling") break;`. For a steady-state connected account the stored status is 'connected', so the loop breaks on iteration 0 — before any listMessages (line 81) or listChatAttendees (line 64) call. The only paid call that runs is the single pre-loop listChats at line 36. Thus each rebackfill = ~1 paid Unipile call, not the claimed ~260, and the 'two concurrent rebackfills both loop 200 pages' scenario is refuted because neither sets the store status. By contrast, notify/route.ts:35 and accounts/route.ts:39 DO create the connection with status 'backfilling' in the store, which is why their loops actually page — rebackfill is the outlier that omits this. Residual real gaps: (1) no rate limit / in-flight guard, so a scripted loop still burns N paid listChats calls at 1x each, while the team already applies exactly this pattern to paid fan-out in enrich/person/route.ts:12-22 (10/min/device); (2) a narrow, self-terminating race — a rebackfill fired during an account's one-time initial backfill window (store status genuinely 'backfilling') would run its loop and page concurrently, but the first loop to reach setConnectionStatus(...,'connected',1) at line 148 flips status and forces all others to bail at line 80. Net: the endpoint genuinely lacks a rate limit and dedup, but the existing store-status guard neuters the claimed 200-page/260-call amplification in steady state, dropping severity from high to low. Not covered by the plan's Phase 0 elaboration (systemCore/Safety/auth only), so planStatus unaddressed for the residual minor issue.
refined: rebackfill lacks a rate limit/in-flight guard, but because it only publishes an SSE 'backfilling' event and never sets the store status, the backfill loop's status guard (backfill.ts:80) bails on iteration 0 for a connected account — so each call costs just one paid listChats (backfill.ts:36), not ~260, leaving only a low-severity 1x paid-call spam plus a narrow, self-terminating double-paging race when a rebackfill overlaps an account's one-time initial backfill window.

title: The 30/day autodraft cap lives only in the Mac client's UserDefaults — no server enforcement for Pro
mechanism: AutodraftPolicy.dailyCap=30 is a pure client-side guard whose counter is persisted in the app's UserDefaults/autodraftCap.json. The server has no autodraft-specific cap, and for Pro the shared quota is unlimited. So deleting/rolling the local cap file, a client bug, or a modified client removes the only limiter, and each autodraft is a full Sonnet suggest call fired on inbound arrival.
breaksAt: Any Pro user whose local cap state is bypassed — server sees ordinary unlimited Pro suggest traffic and does not distinguish or bound autodraft.
evidence: Sources/OsmoShell/AutodraftPolicy.swift:16 (dailyCap 30, pure/client), App/Sources/App/AppModel.swift:827-836 (cap in UserDefaults/JSON), :853-874 (decision then service.suggest)
reasoning: Mechanism verified. AutodraftPolicy.dailyCap=30 is pure client-side (Sources/OsmoShell/AutodraftPolicy.swift:16) and gated on isPro (line 40). The counter persists in an on-disk JSON file autodraftCap.json in supportDir (App/Sources/App/AppModel.swift:829-837), decremented around the decide->service.suggest flow (853-874). Nit: it's a supportDir JSON file, not UserDefaults (only autodraftEnabled is in UserDefaults, 800-803) — but the point (a local, user-deletable file is the only counter) stands. Server side confirms the gap: web/app/api/suggest/route.ts:57-76 applies quota only via checkAndConsume, and for Pro/trial quota.ts:29 returns early {allowed:true, remaining:null} with NO counting and NO ceiling; there is no autodraft-specific cap and no general per-device rate limit on /api/suggest (the only rate limiter, /api/enrich/person, is a different endpoint). So the server truly cannot distinguish or bound autodraft traffic for Pro. Not mitigated. Severity revised down from medium to low: the client cap is a UX/cost throttle, never a security boundary; the real underlying gap is the absence of ANY server-side cost ceiling for Pro/trial, of which autodraft is just one (automatic, loopable) manifestation — the interactive suggest path is equally unbounded for Pro, so autodraft adds only modest incremental risk. Exploitation requires an unlocked Pro/trial entitlement, and the blast radius is the shared Anthropic bill/margin with no data or auth impact. Plan unaddressed: Phase 0 elaboration only covers the systemCore/Safety/auth fix, and no phase introduces a per-Pro server cost ceiling (Phase 6's evidence-gated ceiling concerns quality, not cost).
refined: For Pro/trial devices the /api/suggest proxy applies no server-side ceiling or even usage counting (checkAndConsume returns early when unlimited), so the client-side 30/day cap in autodraftCap.json is the only throttle on automatic, unmetered Sonnet calls — a shared-Anthropic-bill/margin (not data/auth) risk that a buggy or modified Pro/trial client removes, though the interactive suggest path is equally unbounded.

title: cache_control:ephemeral on every systemCore pays the cache-write premium even for low-reuse call sites
mechanism: The route unconditionally marks the client-sent systemCore as cache_control ephemeral. Anthropic prompt-cache writes cost ~1.25x base input tokens and reads ~0.1x, with a short (5-min) TTL. Each of the 7 call sites composes a DIFFERENT systemCore (suggest/threadIntel/dossier/ask/insight/voicePersona), so infrequent sites (dossier, ask, voicePersona) rarely get a within-TTL read hit — they pay the 25% write premium on the large core with little/no reuse benefit, a net cost increase versus not caching. Any accidental volatile content in systemCore also forces a full-price miss every time.
breaksAt: Steadily on the long tail of infrequent call sites; magnitude scales with how large each site's systemCore is and how sparse its traffic.
evidence: web/app/api/suggest/route.ts:81-84 (unconditional ephemeral cache on systemCore); 7 distinct client-composed cores e.g. Sources/OsmoBrain/ThreadIntel.swift:42, Sources/OsmoBrain/Dossier.swift:51
reasoning: The mechanism misreads how Anthropic prompt caching prices sub-minimum prefixes. web/app/api/suggest/route.ts:83 does unconditionally mark systemCore as cache_control:ephemeral, and 7 distinct static cores route through this single endpoint (verified: PromptComposer/suggest, ThreadIntel.swift:42, Dossier.swift:52, Ask.swift:31, VoiceStats.swift:125, Insight.swift:31, MessageJudge.swift:30, each posting prompt.systemCore via ClaudeProxyGenerator.generate). But the claim's cost harm ("pay the 25% write premium on the large core with little/no reuse benefit, a net cost increase versus not caching") does not occur. Per the Anthropic caching reference, a block below the model's minimum cacheable prefix (1024 tok Sonnet 4.5-tier / 2048 Sonnet 4.6 / 4096 Opus) silently does NOT cache — cache_creation_input_tokens:0, no error, and it is billed at NORMAL input price, i.e. NO write premium. The low-frequency sites the claim specifically names are the SMALLEST cores: Ask ~510 chars (~128 tok), VoiceStats/voicePersona ~802 chars (~200 tok), Dossier ~1000 chars (~250 tok) — all far below any minimum. So cache_control on them is an inert no-op with zero cost delta, not a net cost increase. The only core that approaches the ~1024-tok Sonnet threshold is the suggest core (PromptComposer literal ~2781 chars + AntiTell.block ~1300 chars ≈ ~1000-1360 tok), and suggest is the highest-frequency interactive draft path — exactly where a within-TTL read hit pays off, the opposite of the claim's low-reuse framing. The secondary "accidental volatile content" worry is speculative and already guarded: ThreadIntel.swift:80-82 explicitly keeps time-varying content (TODAY IS) in the userTurn, noting it must not touch systemCore "or every thread would miss the prompt cache," and all 7 cores are compile-time static constants. Net: no write premium is charged on the targeted sites, so there is no cost weakness — the finding rests on treating a below-minimum ephemeral marker as if it incurred the 1.25x write cost, which it does not. Plan status is moot since the finding is not real; the plan does not address per-call-site cache tiering, but there is nothing to fix.
refined: Refuted: the six low-frequency systemCores are only ~128-375 tokens, far below the 1024-2048-token minimum cacheable prefix, so cache_control:ephemeral is a silent no-op billed at normal input price (no 1.25x write premium and thus no net cost increase); only the ~1k-token suggest core nears the threshold and it is the highest-reuse site, so the claimed write-premium-on-low-reuse mechanism does not exist.

title: In-memory oplog + dedup map grow unboundedly with full message content on ONE 512MB instance → OOM is the first thing to fall over
mechanism: appendRows() is strictly append-only: it pushes full WireContact/WireThread/WireMessage row objects (including message TEXT) onto oplogs.get(deviceId) and never truncates, compacts, or evicts (memoryStore.ts:206-229). A parallel dedup Map holds a sha1 hex per native key (memoryStore.ts:124,214-215), so every device carries TWO in-heap copies of its entire history's key space. Nothing frees this except account deletion (purgeDevice, :292). Backfill imports up to MAX_MESSAGE_PAGES 200 x 250/page = ~50k messages per account (backfill.ts:17-18) at OSMO_BACKFILL_SCOPE=full/60d (render.yaml:28), across gmail+slack+x+linkedin+whatsapp+instagram per device. All of this stays resident on the single Render 'starter' instance (render.yaml:13, ~512MB). Node heap fills and the process is OOM-killed.
breaksAt: Low hundreds of active devices with real multi-platform histories, or even one power user with a large WhatsApp/Gmail account, exhausts 512MB. OOM -> Render restart -> mass re-register/re-backfill -> re-hash everything -> faster re-OOM.
reasoning: Code confirms the core mechanism. appendRows (memoryStore.ts:206-229) is strictly append-only — it pushes full WireMessage/WireThread/WireContact rows (message text included) onto oplogs.get(deviceId) via oplog.push({seq,kind,row}) with no cap, ring buffer, TTL, or compaction. pull() (:231-247) never removes delivered entries — retention is intentional so a device that resets its cursor can re-pull idempotently (the 'restart amnesia by design' comment, :6-8), meaning even a fully-synced device keeps its entire 60-day history resident forever. Grep across lib/connections and app confirms NO splice/trim/evict/size-cap logic anywhere; the only removal is purgeDevice (:292-308), called solely from /api/account/delete. Backfill imports up to MAX_MESSAGE_PAGES 200×250=50k msgs/account (backfill.ts:18) at full/60d scope (render.yaml:28) across 6 platforms, all resident on ONE 512MB starter instance (render.yaml:13). Beyond backfill, /api/webhooks/unipile (webhooks/unipile/route.ts:29) appends inbound messages via appendRows for the whole process lifetime, so heap grows in steady state too — a vector the claim understated. OOM kills the single instance, wiping ALL in-memory state (oplog, OAuth tokens, quota, SSE), and the app's re-register-and-re-pull design (memoryStore.ts:6-8) triggers a thundering-herd re-backfill that rebuilds the same over-budget footprint (plus O(total) work per pull/hash), producing a crash loop. This is a whole-service availability failure triggered by normal usage with no attacker needed — critical is justified. Minor framing nit: the dedup map holds native-key→sha1-hex (a secondary index), not a second full copy of message text, so 'two in-heap copies of history' overstates; growth is dominated by the oplog rows regardless, so the conclusion is unchanged. Not mitigated in code. planStatus=partial: Phase 0 names 'durable state' and the getStore() Postgres seam (:336) is explicitly built to swap the oplog out of heap, which would fix this, but the plan's Phase 0 elaboration specifies only the systemCore/Safety/auth fix, not the oplog OOM.
refined: The per-device oplog and dedup index (memoryStore.ts:206-229, :124) store full message bodies in the heap append-only with no trimming/compaction/eviction except account deletion (:292), and continue growing via both 50k-msg/account backfills (backfill.ts:18) and lifelong webhook ingest (webhooks/unipile:29), so on the single 512MB starter instance (render.yaml:13) cumulative resident memory grows unbounded until OOM wipes all in-memory server state and the app's auto-re-backfill design (memoryStore.ts:6-8) drives a crash loop.

title: pull() does two O(n) synchronous scans of the whole oplog per request on a single 0.5-vCPU event loop; SSE doorbells make pulls frequent
mechanism: pull() runs oplog.filter(e => e.seq > since) over the ENTIRE array then a second oplog.some(e => e.seq > last) for hasMore (memoryStore.ts:231-247) with no index on seq and no binary search on the sorted array. This is synchronous CPU on Node's single thread. Every sync.dirty doorbell (published once per backfill page, per send, per inbound) plus the app's reconciliation timer triggers a /api/sync/pull, and each pull re-scans a possibly-50k-entry array. appendRows also hashes every row via JSON.stringify+sha1 synchronously (:144-145,214). Because the instance is 0.5 vCPU and single-threaded, concurrent large pulls + concurrent backfill hashing serialize on one event loop; latency for ALL routes (SSE heartbeats, /api/suggest, /api/health) balloons behind them.
breaksAt: Tens of concurrent pulls against multi-thousand-entry oplogs, or a backfill spike, saturates the single vCPU; Render's health check can then time out -> restart -> stampede.
reasoning: Every code-level assertion checks out: pull() (memoryStore.ts:233) does an O(n) oplog.filter(e=>e.seq>since).slice(0,limit) — allocating the whole matching tail before slicing — with no seq index/binary search, then a second scan oplog.some(e=>e.seq>last) at :245 for hasMore that fails to short-circuit (walks the full array to return false) in the common drained-to-tail case. hash() at :144-145 is synchronous sha1(JSON.stringify(row)) per row in appendRows(:213). Single-thread, plan:starter=0.5vCPU (render.yaml:13). Doorbells are un-debounced and frequent (backfill.ts:141,151; send/route.ts:64; webhooks/unipile:30), each driving a pull. No oplog length cap anywhere, so the array grows unbounded. So the mechanism is REAL and un-mitigated (no index, binary search, debounce, or worker thread). BUT severity high is overstated: the scans are per-device (bounded by one device's history, not a shared global array), the inner op is a trivial integer compare (sub-ms even at 50k entries on 0.5vCPU), and backfill hashing interleaves with network-bound Unipile page fetches that yield the loop, so CPU rarely serializes into saturation. The claimed cascade (all-route latency balloon -> health-check timeout -> restart stampede) is speculative at starter/free-launch concurrency; the plan itself says bump to standard for real traffic. At the scale where this would bite, unbounded oplog memory in a 512MB instance is the likelier first failure than these CPU scans. planStatus partial: Phase 0 names durable state and the store is an explicit swappable seam (Postgres query would index seq and fix the scan incidentally), but the Phase 0 elaboration is scoped only to systemCore/Safety/auth and specifies no indexing/pagination/pull-perf work.
refined: pull() and its hasMore check are genuine un-indexed O(n) synchronous full-array scans (plus per-row synchronous sha1 in appendRows) on a single 0.5-vCPU event loop — a real, un-mitigated algorithmic inefficiency and unbounded per-heavy-user tax — but because the scans are per-device with a trivial constant factor and interleave with I/O, the absolute cost is sub-millisecond and the claimed all-route-latency-balloon/health-timeout/restart-stampede cascade is speculative, with unbounded oplog memory in 512MB being the more probable first failure.

title: SSE doorbell fanout is a per-process globalThis map with a per-connection setInterval — it hard-caps the system at ONE instance and leaks timers on abrupt disconnects
mechanism: Subscribers live in a globalThis Map<deviceId,Set<Controller>> (events.ts:10-17); publish() only reaches controllers in THIS process (:26-34). Because the oplog seq counter and the doorbell publisher are both per-process, a second instance is architecturally impossible: a sync.dirty published on instance A never reaches a device's SSE stream pinned to instance B, so you cannot horizontally scale to relieve the OOM/CPU pressure above — the only lever is a bigger single box, which has a ceiling. Every device also holds ONE always-open SSE connection continuously (not just while drafting), each with a 25s setInterval heartbeat (events.ts:56-59). The interval is cleared ONLY in cancel() (:61-65); if the client TCP drops without a clean cancel (common behind proxies/laptop sleep), the controller stays in the Set with its heartbeat interval firing forever — a slow timer+socket leak that compounds until a publish() happens to hit it and throw.
breaksAt: Thousands of concurrent devices = thousands of persistent sockets + timers on one 0.5-vCPU/512MB box; and the moment you try to add a 2nd Render instance to cope, doorbells silently stop delivering to half of users.
reasoning: Both mechanisms are grounded in the cited code. events.ts:10-17 stores subscribers in a per-process globalThis Map; publish() (:26-34) only reaches controllers in the current process, so doorbells cannot cross instances — confirmed. But this single-instance property is the system's DOCUMENTED intended architecture ("ONE always-on instance," oplog seq/OAuth/quota/links all in-memory), so SSE is not a uniquely-discovered cap; the actionable risk is a silent-failure landmine if anyone scales to a 2nd instance. The timer leak is also real and unmitigated: clearInterval runs only in cancel() (:62); the heartbeat catch (:58) is empty and does not prune or clear; publish() prunes the set on throw (:32) but has no reference to the heartbeat closure so it never clears the timer. I grepped all of web/: there is no req.signal/abort handler (route.ts:10-25 ignores req.signal) and no reaper, so on an abrupt disconnect where the runtime does not invoke cancel(), the 25s interval plus its retained controller closure leak. Severity revised high->medium: the single-instance cap is intended design rather than a latent bug, each leaked timer is lightweight, and the clean-close path works in the common case, but the absence of any abort/reaper fallback plus a persistent per-user connection on a 0.5vCPU/512MB box is genuine robustness/scaling debt. Plan Phase 0 ("Security & durable state") is elaborated ONLY for systemCore/Safety/auth; SSE fanout and the timer leak are untouched, so unaddressed.
refined: SSE fanout keeps per-device subscriber sets and 25s heartbeat intervals in per-process globalThis state with the interval cleared only in ReadableStream.cancel() and no req.signal/abort fallback or reaper — publish() prunes the set but never clears the timer — so abrupt disconnects leak a live interval plus its retained controller closure, while the per-process publisher means any horizontal scale-out silently drops doorbells to devices pinned to another instance (a real but medium-severity facet of the backend's intended single-instance design).

title: All 7 LLM sites funnel through one blocking, non-streaming, uncapped /api/suggest wire with NO global concurrency or spend cap; the plan's background quality gate 3-4x's the fanout
mechanism: /api/suggest issues a single blocking fetch to Anthropic with no timeout, no retry, no streaming, max_tokens 700 (suggest/route.ts:78-100). There is NO concurrency limiter, semaphore, queue, or global spend circuit-breaker anywhere in web/ (grep confirms none). Each in-flight suggest holds a Node request open for the full multi-second Anthropic latency on the single instance. The demand multiplier is large: threadIntel fires exactly one Claude call per thread per new inbound (ThreadIntel.swift:167-173) and autodraft pre-computes up to dailyCap=30 drafts/day/device (AutodraftPolicy.swift:17) on inbound. Under load Anthropic org RPM/TPM/concurrency limits return 429/529 -> the proxy returns 502 with NO retry (:98-99) -> the Swift client has no retry and GeneratorRouter silently swallows the error and returns MOCK drafts (ClaudeProxyGenerator.swift:79-86), so at scale users silently get '[mock]' text believing it is real. The NEW BACKEND plan's S4 generate-2 -> Haiku judge -> S5 repair adds 3-4 more calls per backgrounded draft, multiplying the very fanout that has no cap.
breaksAt: Aggregate LLM calls exceed the Anthropic org concurrency/TPM cap or the (nonexistent) budget; users start silently getting mock output and cost scales linearly with inbound volume with no ceiling.
reasoning: Structural mechanism is real and well-supported. route.ts:88-96 issues a single blocking, non-streaming fetch to Anthropic with NO signal/AbortController (no timeout), no retry, max_tokens 700; on !res.ok it returns 502 with no retry (:98-99). Grep of web/app and web/lib finds no semaphore/concurrency limiter/queue/circuit-breaker/spend cap (only unrelated setTimeouts). All 7 LLM sites (Insight.swift:133, VoiceStats.swift:218, ThreadIntel.swift:169, SuggestionService.swift:28, MessageJudge.swift:190, Dossier.swift:171, Ask.swift:63) funnel through generator.generate -> ClaudeProxyGenerator -> /api/suggest on the single always-on instance. threadIntel = one call per thread per inbound (ThreadIntel.swift:167-173) and autodraft dailyCap=30/day/device (AutodraftPolicy.swift:17) are confirmed fanout multipliers. HOWEVER the headline consequence is misread: the 429/529->502 path does NOT silently return mock. ClaudeProxyGenerator.swift:57 throws GenerationError.http(502); GeneratorRouter.generate (ClaudeProxyGenerator.swift:79-86) only catches .notConfigured and URLError, so http(502) propagates as a thrown error. The '[mock]' silent-real text actually comes from the server keyless state (route.ts:52-55, no ANTHROPIC_API_KEY -> 200 mockTakes) or the client URLError/notConfigured fallback to MockGenerator (Generator.swift) — not from overload. Also partial mitigation exists: quota.ts checkAndConsume enforces a per-device weekly free cap (FREE_DRAFTS_PER_WEEK=15, route.ts:63-76), bounding free-tier spend, though Pro/trial pass unlimited and there is still NO global concurrency or spend circuit-breaker (so that specific mechanism is genuinely unmitigated). Net: real cost/availability concern (no timeout on a blocking upstream fetch on a 512MB single instance; unbounded aggregate Pro spend) but the scariest 'silent mock at scale' framing is misattributed, so severity is medium not high. Plan: Phase 0 elaboration covers only systemCore/Safety/auth (not concurrency/timeout/spend), and S2 is SINGLE-SHOT interactive while generate-2/judge/repair is BACKGROUND ONLY (partially containing interactive fanout); phase 4 mentions cold-start/outage but proposes no concurrency limiter or spend circuit-breaker and the background gate multiplies calls — so partial.
refined: The single always-on proxy makes blocking, non-streaming Anthropic calls with no request timeout, no retry, and no global concurrency or spend circuit-breaker (only a per-device weekly free-tier quota; Pro unlimited), so aggregate cost and event-loop/memory pressure scale with inbound volume across all 7 call sites — but the claimed '429/529 -> 502 -> silent mock' is wrong, since GeneratorRouter re-throws GenerationError.http(502) and only falls back to mock on notConfigured/URLError.

title: Backfills run as unbounded fire-and-forget promises INSIDE the web process, competing with request serving and lost (not resumed) on every redeploy
mechanism: connect/notify kicks off backfillConnection with `void ...` — no await, no queue, no concurrency limit (connect/notify/route.ts:47). The loop runs inside the same Node process serving pulls/SSE/suggest, doing up to 200 sequential Unipile page fetches + up to 60 attendee fetches, normalizing and sha1-hashing thousands of rows into the in-heap oplog (backfill.ts:76-146). N users connecting near-simultaneously (a launch/marketing spike) spawn N concurrent backfill loops on ONE 0.5-vCPU instance with no admission control, saturating the event loop and Unipile rate limits at once and inflating heap toward OOM. Worse, it is pure in-memory in-process work: autoDeploy:true redeploys on every push to main (render.yaml:17), and any redeploy or OOM mid-backfill discards all progress; the connection is left 'backfilling'/'degraded' (:148-160) and the retry restarts the ENTIRE 50k-row re-page from scratch — amplification, not resume.
breaksAt: Dozens of simultaneous first-time connects, or a single deploy during a backfill wave, saturates CPU/Unipile and forces full re-imports for every interrupted device.
reasoning: Mechanism verified. backfillConnection is dispatched as `void ...` (fire-and-forget, no await/queue/concurrency guard) from three entry points: connect/notify/route.ts:47, accounts/route.ts:48, connect/rebackfill/route.ts:26. Each loop does up to MAX_MESSAGE_PAGES=200 sequential Unipile page fetches (backfill.ts:18,77-146) plus up to MAX_ATTENDEE_FETCHES=60 attendee calls (:59-74), normalizing and sha1-hashing every row into an in-heap oplog + dedup Map (memoryStore.ts:206-229, hash() :144-146). It runs inside the same single always-on 0.5-vCPU/512MB instance that serves pulls/SSE/suggest (render.yaml:8-16), and there is genuinely no admission control, so N simultaneous connects spawn N concurrent import loops. autoDeploy:true (render.yaml:17) plus purely in-heap state means an interrupted backfill loses progress; cursor is never persisted (backfill.ts:76 always starts cursor=undefined), so a re-run re-pages from the start. Existing per-loop safeguards (page/attendee ceilings, user-Stop bail :80, idempotent dedup on platform+messageID :214) cap a SINGLE runaway loop but do nothing about cross-loop concurrency, admission, or resume — so not mitigated. Two reasons to downgrade from high to medium: (1) there is no server-side reconciliation/retry poller (grep found only mock drip and SSE heartbeat timers; adoptOrphanedAccounts is OFF by default, accounts/route.ts:25), so the 'retry re-pages 50k rows' amplification is user-initiated, not automatic, and on redeploy the whole oplog is wiped anyway so the re-page is largely necessary re-population rather than gratuitous waste; (2) the loops are I/O-bound and per-loop-serialized (each awaits Unipile), Unipile rate-limiting self-throttles, and marginal per-loop memory is bounded (250 rows/page, 60 attendees) — the real OOM driver is cumulative oplog volume, which is independent of concurrency — so the CPU-saturation/OOM-via-concurrency framing is inflated. Still a real availability risk (event-loop contention + Unipile burst during onboarding waves on one tiny instance) and the plan (Phase 0 = only systemCore/Safety/auth; Phase 4 cold-start/outage refers to the LLM quality gate, not connection imports) adds no backfill admission control, queue, or cursor persistence — planStatus unaddressed.
refined: In-process history backfills are dispatched fire-and-forget with no concurrency cap or admission control (notify:47, accounts:48, rebackfill:26), so an onboarding wave runs unbounded concurrent 200-page/60-attendee import loops on the single 0.5-vCPU/512MB instance that also serves pulls/SSE/suggest, and because cursors are never persisted and all state is in-heap (wiped by autoDeploy redeploys) any interruption forces a full re-page rather than a resume.

title: Device auth reads ONLY in-memory state, so every restart 401s ALL devices → synchronized re-register stampede that mints new IDs, hammering Supabase and orphaning device rows forever
mechanism: requireDevice() validates the bearer token against getStore().deviceByToken — the in-memory map only (auth.ts:14-22); the durable copy written to Supabase osmo_devices at registration is NEVER consulted for auth. So any restart (OOM, or autoDeploy on any push to main, render.yaml:17) wipes the in-memory devices map and EVERY active device's next request 401s. The Swift client responds by re-registering (BackendClient.swift:330-334 -> register 61-73), and registerDevice() mints a brand-new dev-UUID + token every call (memoryStore.ts:160-168). This produces three compounding scaling problems: (1) a synchronized thundering-herd of /api/device/register + re-pull + SSE-reopen across the whole active base on every deploy; (2) each register does a Supabase upsert with a NEW id (device/register/route.ts:13), so osmo_devices accumulates one orphaned row per device per restart, growing without bound; (3) the old device-owned subscription/trial (keyed to the discarded deviceId) is orphaned, resetting free-tier state. This turns any single failure into a base-wide recovery event that itself drives load toward the next failure.
breaksAt: At 10x users every deploy = 10x simultaneous register writes to Supabase + 10x simultaneous full re-pulls/SSE reconnects; osmo_devices row count grows by (active devices) per deploy indefinitely.
reasoning:
Mechanism holds on every cited line. requireDevice (auth.ts:14-22) resolves the bearer only via getStore().deviceByToken, the in-memory MemoryConnectionsStore.devices map (memoryStore.ts:169), recreated empty per process (memoryStore.ts:337). The durable accounts store is separate; osmo_devices persists the token (store.ts:230-231) but AccountsStore exposes only deviceById, no deviceByToken (store.ts:54), so auth cannot fall back to durable state; any restart 401s every active device. The Swift 401 policy (BackendClient.swift:330-332; SSE loop 238-271) does dropCredentials then register(), and registerDevice() mints a fresh dev-uuid+token each call (memoryStore.ts:160-168). device/register/route.ts:13 upserts with the NEW id (onConflict id), so a new row is inserted each time and old rows are never reclaimed except on account deletion, confirming unbounded osmo_devices growth. render.yaml:17 autoDeploy true fires this base-wide on every commit, at 1x not only 10x. All three sub-claims are real; the sharpest teeth exceed the hammering-Supabase headline: entitlement resolves off subscriptionForDevice(device.id) via ownerFor to deviceById(newId).userId (store.ts:287-289; consumed at license/validate route.ts:22-23 and account/link route.ts:30-31), so a re-registered unlinked deviceId silently returns a signed FREE entitlement, downgrading paying users until they redo Sign in with Apple, and resets unlinked-device trials each deploy. Not mitigated: SSE backoff/jitter (BackendClient.swift:269-271) only slightly spreads the reconnect burst; nothing persists device auth or stabilizes the deviceId, and register/route.ts:12's claim that the durable copy makes the subscription survive restarts is defeated by the id churn. Severity high is warranted: it degrades correctness/billing for the whole base on every deploy at current scale. planStatus partial: Phase 0 names durable state but the elaborated fix only covers the /api/suggest systemCore/Safety/keyless-bearer hole, not persisting device auth or a stable deviceId.</reasoning>
</invoke>
refined: Because device auth reads only the in-memory devices map and the durable osmo_devices copy has no token accessor, every restart (autoDeploy on each commit) 401s the whole active base and forces each client to re-register a brand-new deviceId+token, which base-wide on every deploy silently downgrades linked paying users to Free, resets unlinked-device trials (metering bypass), leaks a permanently orphaned osmo_devices row per device, and bursts register+re-pull+SSE-reconnect at the single 512MB instance.

title: Magic-link verifyUrl is returned in the HTTP response body unconditionally (even with Resend configured) with no rate limit -> full web-account takeover for any email
mechanism: POST /api/auth/request mints a valid 15-min single-use magic-link token and, on EVERY response, returns `verifyUrl` (containing the raw token) in the JSON body. The `if (process.env.RESEND_API_KEY)` block is an empty TODO comment that neither sends the email NOR strips the URL — so even in 'live' mode the token is handed straight back to the caller. Anyone POSTs {email:'victim@x.com'} and receives the exact verify link; GET-ing it (/api/auth/verify) does findOrCreateUserByEmail + createWebSession and sets the osmo_session cookie for that user. That session drives /account and /api/account/upgrade. There is no per-email/IP throttle.
breaksAt: Immediately, for any known email address (emails are public); worse the instant real users exist since it grants their /account portal + subscription controls.
evidence: web/app/api/auth/request/route.ts:22-27 (empty RESEND block; verifyUrl always in body); web/app/api/auth/verify/route.ts:18-23 (consumes token -> session cookie)
reasoning: Code confirms the mechanism verbatim. web/app/api/auth/request/route.ts:18-27: createMagicLink mints a valid 15-min single-use token (store.ts:179-183 in-mem / 337-342 Supabase), verifyUrl embeds the raw token, and line 27 returns verifyUrl in EVERY response. The `if (process.env.RESEND_API_KEY)` block (lines 22-25) is an empty TODO — it does not send the email and does not strip the URL, so even in "sent"/live mode the token is returned to the caller (and since email is never actually sent, the body is the ONLY delivery channel). web/app/api/auth/verify/route.ts:11-23 consumes the token then findOrCreateUserByEmail + createWebSession and sets the osmo_session cookie, which authenticates /account (currentSessionUser, session.ts:38-44) and /api/account/upgrade (upgrade/route.ts:11-14). No mitigation: repo grep shows rate limiting only in app/api/enrich/person/route.ts (device-keyed), no middleware.ts, and no throttle on /api/auth/request; magic_links + web_sessions are durable in Supabase. findOrCreateUserByEmail also auto-provisions accounts for arbitrary emails. Result: any unauthenticated caller who knows a public email obtains a working login link and a session cookie for that account. Severity critical (pre-auth account takeover gated only by a public identifier, no rate limit); the pre-launch/mock deployment state is a real-world dampener but the flaw persists by design into live mode, so it stays critical. Plan status unaddressed: Phase 0's security elaboration is scoped to the /api/suggest systemCore/Safety/keyless-bearer MUST-FIX items and never touches the web magic-link flow.
refined: POST /api/auth/request returns the magic-link verifyUrl (raw single-use token) in the JSON body on every response — the RESEND_API_KEY branch is an empty TODO that neither emails nor strips it — and with no rate limit any unauthenticated attacker can mint and read a valid login token for any email, GET /api/auth/verify to obtain an osmo_session cookie, and fully take over that web account.

title: Every paywall bypass is LIVE in production because Stripe is intentionally omitted, so all `if (STRIPE_SECRET_KEY)` guards evaluate false
mechanism: render.yaml deliberately ships NO Stripe key ('Stripe intentionally omitted — launching free-first'). The 'disabled once a real Stripe key is present' safety on the dev/mock grant routes therefore never engages in prod. GET /api/checkout/mock-complete?device=<id> has NO auth and flips subscriptionActive:true for any deviceId that exists in the accounts DB (every registered device); /api/license/reset stays live; /api/account/upgrade takes its mock branch and activates Pro for any web session. /api/checkout/session hands the app a mock-complete URL. Chained with the open /api/device/register, an attacker self-serves unlimited Pro.
breaksAt: In production now — the very configuration Osmo plans to launch with (no Stripe key) is what leaves these routes armed.
evidence: web/render.yaml:63 (Stripe omitted) + :26-27 (OSMO_REQUIRE_AUTH=1 only); web/app/api/checkout/mock-complete/route.ts:9,19 (gate off in keyless, grants Pro, no auth); web/app/api/account/upgrade/route.ts:16-24
reasoning: No STRIPE_SECRET_KEY in prod (render.yaml, intentional) so all Stripe guards are false. mock-complete route is unauthenticated (no requireDevice), only checks deviceById at line 15 then sets subscriptionActive true at line 19; device register is open. resolveTier (entitlement.ts:20) maps that to pro, so license validate returns a real server-signed pro entitlement. suggest route:69 plus quota.ts treat pro as unlimited, nullifying the FREE_DRAFTS_PER_WEEK 15 guard that protects the Anthropic bill. setSubscriptionForDevice (store.ts:296) persists it in Supabase, surviving restart and the future Stripe cutover. Not mitigated: the guards are disarmed by the deliberate key omission. Plan Phase 0 only fixes the suggest route, so this is unaddressed.
refined: With no STRIPE_SECRET_KEY in the free-first prod config, the unauthenticated mock-complete route durably grants pro to any registered device, bypassing the Anthropic-cost quota and persisting past the eventual Stripe cutover.

title: Any license key beginning 'OSMO-' is accepted as an active Pro subscription
mechanism: validateLicenseKey() uppercases/trims and returns {valid:true, plan:'com.osmo.pro.monthly'} for anything starting 'OSMO-'. POST /api/license/validate (device Bearer only, obtainable via open register) then setSubscriptionForDevice({subscriptionActive:true}) and returns a server-SIGNED entitlement the app fully trusts. This is independent of Stripe, so it works even after real Stripe keys are added.
breaksAt: Immediately and permanently (survives adding Stripe), affecting all revenue.
reasoning: Mechanism holds exactly as cited. validateLicenseKey (web/lib/license/entitlement.ts:45-49) returns {valid:true, plan:"com.osmo.pro.monthly"} for any key that .trim().toUpperCase().startsWith("OSMO-"), with no env/NODE_ENV/live guard. POST /api/license/validate (web/app/api/license/validate/route.ts:16-23) requires only a device Bearer (requireDevice, auth.ts:14-22), and a token is trivially obtained keyless via POST /api/device/register (register/route.ts:9-19, "Always succeeds keyless"). On a valid key it calls setSubscriptionForDevice({subscriptionActive:true,...}) (:19) and returns buildSignedEntitlement (:23); resolveTier maps subscriptionActive->tier "pro" (entitlement.ts:19) and signEntitlement produces a genuine Ed25519 server signature (sign.ts) the app fully trusts — not a forgery. Decisive corroboration for the "survives Stripe" claim: the sibling /api/license/reset route IS gated `if (process.env.STRIPE_SECRET_KEY) return 404` (reset/route.ts:10-12), proving the codebase has a live-mode guard pattern, yet /api/license/validate deliberately lacks it, so wiring real Stripe (STRIPE_SECRET_KEY / webhook) disables reset but leaves the OSMO- bypass fully active. No existing code mitigates it. Not addressed by the plan: Phase 0 MUST-FIX targets /api/suggest systemCore/Safety/keyless-bearer; this is a separate license-validation logic flaw (auth itself succeeds) and is nowhere in the plan. Only nuance: this is clearly pre-launch mock code (billing is all stubs — mock-complete checkout, MOCK-WEB upgrade, stripe webhook no-op), but the guard-on-reset/no-guard-on-validate asymmetry makes it a genuine gap that ships to production silently, justifying critical.
refined: Anyone can keyless-register a device token via POST /api/device/register, then POST /api/license/validate {licenseKey:"OSMO-..."} to receive a genuine server-Ed25519-signed "pro" entitlement, because validateLicenseKey accepts any OSMO-prefixed string and — unlike the STRIPE_SECRET_KEY-gated /api/license/reset — has no live-mode guard, so the free-Pro bypass persists even after real Stripe is wired.

title: /api/account/link performs NO Apple JWT verification and trusts a client-supplied email, enabling subscription theft and account hijack from a victim's email alone
mechanism: The route trusts body.appleUserID and body.email verbatim. findOrCreateUserByApple: when no user matches the (attacker-invented) appleUserID, it falls back to matching the supplied email; if a user row already exists for that email (from a prior Apple sign-in or web magic-link login), it OVERWRITES that user's apple_user_id with the attacker's value and returns the victim's user. account/link then linkDeviceToUser(attackerDevice, victimUser), so subscriptionForDevice(attackerDevice) now resolves to the victim's Pro subscription and returns a signed Pro entitlement. It also repoints the victim's apple_user_id, and the attacker can then POST /api/account/delete (device Bearer) to purge the victim's user, subs, sessions and all their devices.
breaksAt: As soon as a victim has an account row (any Apple sign-in or web login). Emails are effectively public, so blast radius = every account.
evidence: web/app/api/account/link/route.ts:15-31 (no Apple token verify); web/lib/accounts/store.ts:271-275 (email branch overwrites apple_user_id, returns existing user); web/app/api/account/delete/route.ts:12-14 (device -> full user purge)
reasoning: Mechanism holds end-to-end on the live (Supabase) path and the in-memory path. (1) Keyless entry: POST /api/device/register mints a device token with no auth (device/register/route.ts:9-19); requireDevice only checks that token exists in the store (auth.ts:14-22). (2) No identity verification: account/link/route.ts:15-24 parses body.appleUserID/email/fullName and passes them straight to findOrCreateUserByApple — no Apple identityToken/JWT/JWKS/nonce/audience check anywhere. Confirmed no jose/jsonwebtoken/apple-signin dependency in web/package.json; grep for apple/jwt/jwks/nonce finds only this route, mock-complete, and store.ts (no verification). (3) Email-fallback overwrite: SupabaseAccountsStore.findOrCreateUserByApple (store.ts:266-275) queries by the attacker's invented apple_user_id (no match), then falls back to matching the supplied email and UPDATEs that existing (victim) row's apple_user_id to the attacker value, returning the victim user — identical logic in the memory store (store.ts:133-140). (4) Subscription theft: linkDeviceToUser sets attacker device.user_id = victim.id (store.ts:243-244); subscriptionForDevice then resolves owner via device.user_id → user:victim and returns the victim's user-level subscription (store.ts:287-294), and buildSignedEntitlement issues a signed tier:pro entitlement (entitlement.ts:19,28-38) back to the attacker. (5) Destruction: POST /api/account/delete with the attacker Bearer calls purgeByDevice, which — because the attacker device now points at the victim user — deletes the victim's subscription, all of the victim's devices, and the victim user row (delete/route.ts:11-14; store.ts:362-372). Precondition (victim has an account row) is trivially met by any prior Apple sign-in OR any web magic-link login (auth/verify/route.ts:18 findOrCreateUserByEmail), and emails are effectively public, so blast radius is every account. No existing code mitigates this. Plan does not cover it: Phase-0 MUST-FIX enumerates only /api/suggest (systemCore verbatim, no Safety re-run, keyless bearer); this is a distinct identity-verification gap in account/link, so planStatus is unaddressed. Severity critical is correct: unauthenticated full account takeover + Pro theft + irreversible destruction of arbitrary accounts, keyed on a public email.
refined: /api/account/link performs no Apple identity-token verification and hands the client-supplied appleUserID+email to findOrCreateUserByApple, whose email-fallback branch (store.ts:270-275 Supabase / 136-140 memory) overwrites an existing user's apple_user_id and returns that victim user, so any attacker with a keyless-registered device plus the victim's public email can link their device to the victim's account, receive a signed Pro entitlement, and then irreversibly purge the victim's account/devices via /api/account/delete.

title: Unauthenticated, unthrottled /api/device/register + per-DEVICE quota/trial = infinite fresh trials and unlimited Anthropic spend
mechanism: POST /api/device/register mints a device token with no auth, no rate limit, no proof-of-work. The free-tier weekly quota (checkAndConsume) and the 14-day trial (startTrialForDevice) are both keyed to deviceId, and /api/suggest identifies the payer by device token. So an attacker loops register -> startTrial (or just register per 15 drafts) to mint unbounded 'trial'/free identities, each granting unmetered or reset LLM access. This directly attacks the crown-jewel Anthropic bill, which has no global spend cap.
breaksAt: At any scale an attacker chooses; each device = a new 14-day unlimited trial or a fresh 15/week bucket.
evidence: web/app/api/device/register/route.ts:9-19 (open, unlimited); web/lib/license/quota.ts:28-38 (keyed per deviceId); web/app/api/trial/start/route.ts:8-12
reasoning: Every link in the chain is confirmed in code. web/app/api/device/register/route.ts:9-19 mints a device via getStore().registerDevice() (memoryStore.ts:160-168: fresh uuid + random token) with zero auth, rate limit, or proof-of-work — always succeeds. The free quota (quota.ts:28-38) is keyed on deviceId (15/week), so each new device is a fresh bucket (and the counter is in-memory, wiped on restart). /api/trial/start (route.ts:8-12) calls startTrialForDevice(device.id); its idempotency guard (store.ts:163-166) only blocks the SAME device, so any freshly minted device gets a new 14-day trial, which resolveTier (entitlement.ts:20-22) maps to tier 'trial' → checkAndConsume treats non-free as unlimited (quota.ts:29). requireDevice (auth.ts:14-22) accepts any token register just issued. /api/suggest (route.ts:61-69) bills the server-held Anthropic key per device. So looping register(+trial/start) mints unbounded unlimited/free identities against the crown-jewel Anthropic bill. No mitigation exists: no middleware.ts, no CAPTCHA/PoW, no global spend cap; the only rate limiter (enrich/person/route.ts:16-39) is itself keyed per-device and doesn't cover /api/suggest. Even under OSMO_REQUIRE_AUTH=1 the attacker registers first to get a valid token. Severity high (denial-of-wallet on the most sensitive resource, no cap), not critical — impact requires a live ANTHROPIC_API_KEY (keyless returns mocks) and per-unit-time throughput is throttled by the single ~512MB always-on instance forwarding each call synchronously, though cumulative spend is unbounded. planStatus partial: Phase 0 'Security & durable state' owns this area and its auth MUST-FIX marginally raises the bar (forces a token), but the elaboration only specifies systemCore/Safety/auth — it adds no registration throttle, account-gated trial, or global spend cap, so the specific abuse vector is essentially unaddressed.
refined: /api/device/register mints device tokens with no auth/throttle/PoW while both the 15/week free quota and the 14-day 'trial' (unlimited) are keyed per deviceId, so a script can loop register(+trial/start) to mint unbounded fresh identities and drive uncapped Anthropic spend, since no global spend cap or registration rate limit exists anywhere.

title: /api/suggest trusts client systemCore verbatim and an unvalidated model id, with auth reduced to mere token-presence
mechanism: In prod (OSMO_REQUIRE_AUTH=1) auth only checks that the bearer maps to SOME registered device — trivially satisfied by open register. body.systemCore is forwarded verbatim as the Anthropic cache_control system block (the named prompt-injection / safety-bypass hole: no server Safety re-run), and body.model is passed straight to Anthropic with no allow-list, so a caller can select an arbitrarily expensive model on Osmo's key. Keyless mode returns a mock and bypasses the quota path entirely.
breaksAt: Immediately; systemCore hole is the plan's headline MUST-FIX, but the model-id trust and presence-only auth are separate live gaps.
evidence: web/app/api/suggest/route.ts:30-49 (presence-only auth, unvalidated model default), :78-86 (model + systemCore forwarded verbatim, no Safety re-run)
reasoning: All three sub-mechanisms verified against source. (1) Presence-only auth: suggest/route.ts:31-36 only checks getStore().deviceByToken(token) maps to a registered device; POST /api/device/register (register/route.ts:9-20) has NO auth and always mints a token via registerDevice() (memoryStore.ts:160-169), so the gate is trivially satisfied by open registration. (2) systemCore hole: :44 reads body.systemCore and :83 forwards it verbatim as the cache_control system text block; a grep of web/ finds NO server-side Safety/moderation module, confirming Safety is never re-run. (3) model-id trust: :46 defaults body.model to 'claude-sonnet-5' but :79 passes any client-supplied model straight to Anthropic with no allow-list. (4) Keyless mock (:51-55) returns before the quota block (:57-76), but this is harmless since keyless = no ANTHROPIC_API_KEY = no real bill. Not mitigated: quota.ts caps free devices at 15 drafts/week and max_tokens is 700, which partially bounds raw spend, but does nothing for the arbitrary system-prompt safety-bypass, does not constrain model choice, treats pro/trial as unlimited, and is defeatable by registering many devices. Severity high (not critical) because quota + token cap bound worst-case spend; the real teeth are prompt-injection/safety-bypass on the paid key plus expensive-model selection. PlanStatus partial: the plan's Phase-0 MUST-FIX explicitly covers systemCore/Safety re-run and the keyless/auth gap, but never mentions a model-id allow-list, leaving that sub-gap unaddressed.
refined: In prod, /api/suggest gates on mere device-token presence (trivially obtained via the unauthenticated /api/device/register) and then forwards the client's systemCore verbatim as the Anthropic system block with no server Safety re-run and passes an unvalidated model id straight through, letting any caller drive Osmo's paid key with an arbitrary system prompt and an arbitrarily expensive model.

title: Long-lived device token accepted via ?token= query for ALL device-authed endpoints, leaking a full credential into logs/referrers
mechanism: requireDevice() falls back to reading the token from the URL query string for every route that uses it — not just SSE. Because the same helper backs POST/DELETE routes (account/delete, sync/send, license/validate, media, etc.), a caller can authenticate any destructive action via ?token=. Query strings are captured by Render access logs, upstream proxies, browser history and Referer headers, so a single leaked URL yields replayable full device auth (the token is a long-lived Keychain secret, not a short-lived session).
breaksAt: Whenever a token-bearing URL is logged or forwarded — routine in production behind Render + CDNs.
evidence: web/lib/connections/auth.ts:16-18 (query-string token fallback, used by every requireDevice route)
reasoning: Mechanism is factually true. web/lib/connections/auth.ts:14-22: requireDevice() takes the token from `Authorization: Bearer`, else falls back to `new URL(req.url).searchParams.get("token")`. The comment calls it an "SSE browser-debug fallback," but the code applies it unconditionally, and requireDevice backs ~20 routes including destructive POST/DELETE ones (grep confirms account/delete/route.ts:11, sync/send:16, license/reset:14, license/validate:12, media/route.ts:41, promo/redeem:22, checkout/session:11, etc.). The device token is a long-lived secret (memoryStore.ts:163 crypto.randomBytes(24) base64url, kept in Keychain, re-registered only on 401, also upserted to durable Supabase), so a leaked token is replayable against every endpoint. So the "over-broad query-string fallback" claim is real. HOWEVER the claimed HIGH-severity impact — "routine in production... captured by Render access logs, referrers, browser history" — is not supported by the code. The Mac app never puts the token in a URL: every call uses the Authorization header, including the SSE doorbell (BackendClient.swift:242-243 sets `Bearer <deviceToken>` on GET /api/events; also :308, SyncCoordinator.swift:99/133, APIClients.swift:56/71/78). A codebase-wide search finds NO place that constructs a `?token=<deviceToken>` URL for these routes — the only `?token=` URLs are magic-link web login (a separate single-use token, auth/verify:9, auth/request:20). These are API calls not page navigations, so there are no Referer/history leaks either. Thus there is no routine production leak path; exploitation requires the long-lived token to first be placed into a URL by a debug session or an already-compromised caller. That makes it a genuine defense-in-depth footgun, not an actively-leaking high vuln. No existing code restricts the fallback to the GET/SSE route, so it is unmitigated. Plan Phase 0's elaboration only covers the systemCore/Safety/keyless-bearer MUST-FIX and does not name this item, so it is unaddressed.
refined: requireDevice() (web/lib/connections/auth.ts:18) accepts the long-lived device token via ?token= on ALL routes including destructive POST/DELETE — a real but low-severity defense-in-depth footgun, since no code path (not even the SSE client, which uses Authorization: Bearer at BackendClient.swift:242) ever places the token in a URL, so the claimed routine log/referrer leak does not occur; fix by restricting the query-string fallback to the GET /api/events route or removing it.

title: Committed dev Ed25519 signing keypair is the fallback signer -> forgeable Pro entitlements if the env key is ever unset
mechanism: sign.ts hardcodes DEV_PRIVATE_D/DEV_PUBLIC_X and privateKeyObject() uses them whenever OSMO_LICENSE_PRIVATE_D/OSMO_LICENSE_PUBLIC_X are absent. The app bundles the matching public key to verify entitlements offline; if the shipped app bundles the DEV public key (or the server boots without the secret env, which render.yaml marks sync:false = not guaranteed set), anyone with the committed private key can forge a Pro/trial SignedEntitlement the app accepts with no server contact. A single misconfigured deploy silently downgrades the entire trust root to a public key.
breaksAt: On any deploy/env-drift where the signing secret is missing; offline entitlement verification means it is undetectable server-side.
evidence: web/lib/license/sign.ts:17-18 (committed keypair), :38-42 (env-or-DEV fallback used for real signing); web/render.yaml:37-40 (key only sync:false, not guaranteed)
reasoning:
The forgery mechanism fails because the committed dev keypair is not the app trust root. sign.ts line 17-18 commits dev public key 4Y5MSU2c and private key sEbPIOa3, but the shipped app hardcodes a DIFFERENT production public key at EntitlementVerifier.swift line 34 (lRI-n0RMoS). The dev key string exists only under web (sign.ts plus tests), never in App or Sources. A signature made with the committed dev private key verifies only against the dev public key; the app verify at EntitlementVerifier.swift line 49-69 checks the production key and returns nil on mismatch, and AppModel.applyEntitlement at AppModel.swift line 214-221 treats nil as Free. So a forged pro payload signed with the committed d is rejected by the shipped app. The alternate path where the server boots without OSMO_LICENSE_PRIVATE_D fails SAFE not open: the server then signs with the dev key, whose signatures the app also rejects, downgrading ALL users including legitimate Pro to Free, a detectable availability annoyance, the opposite of silent privilege escalation. Existing code already prevents the forgery. Residual concern is only hygiene of a committed dev private key plus env-drift causing a fail-safe downgrade, both low; render.yaml line 37-40 sync false does not create a forgery path.</reasoning>
</invoke>
refined: Refuted: the committed dev private key only forges signatures for the dev public key, which the shipped app never bundles; the app trusts a distinct production public key (EntitlementVerifier.swift line 34), so dev-key or key-unset signatures fail verification and fail SAFE to Free rather than forging Pro, leaving only committed-dev-key hygiene and a detectable fail-safe self-downgrade as low residual issues.

title: Stripe webhook does no signature verification AND writes to an ephemeral store that the entitlement path never reads
mechanism: Two compounding bugs: (1) With STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET set, the handler still JSON.parses the raw body directly (constructEvent is a TODO), so any unauthenticated caller can forge checkout.session.completed / subscription.deleted for any client_reference_id. (2) It writes via getStore().setLicense() into the in-memory connections store, but /api/license/validate resolves tier from the SEPARATE durable accounts store (subscriptionForDevice) — the two are disconnected, so real Stripe payments would never grant Pro and forged events flip a value nothing reads. Net: billing is both spoofable and non-functional.
breaksAt: The moment Stripe is wired for real revenue; today it silently no-ops.
evidence: web/app/api/webhooks/stripe/route.ts:15-33 (JSON.parse, no constructEvent, writes getStore().setLicense); vs web/app/api/license/validate/route.ts:22 reading getAccounts().subscriptionForDevice
reasoning: Both cited code facts hold. web/app/api/webhooks/stripe/route.ts:15-18 has constructEvent as a TODO and does JSON.parse(raw) with no signature check and no auth, so with STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET set a forged JSON event is accepted. route.ts:22,29-37 writes via getStore().setLicense() into ConnectionsStore.licenses (memoryStore.ts:269), a store whose license() value is read by NO entitlement path (grep shows setLicense only called by this webhook; readers of tier all use getAccounts().subscriptionForDevice at suggest/route.ts:67, license/validate/route.ts:22, account/link, promo/redeem). The correct working checkout path (checkout/mock-complete/route.ts:19) writes to getAccounts().setSubscriptionForDevice, confirming the webhook targets the wrong store. So bug#1 (unsigned) and bug#2 (disconnected) are both real. However the claimed medium 'spoofable + non-functional' overstates current impact: the two bugs cancel for exploitability. Without the secret the endpoint is a pure no-op (route.ts:13); even with the secret, a forged event lands in a dead store that no entitlement path reads, so the forgery grants zero Pro — there is no live free-Pro escalation today. It is explicitly scaffold code ('Scaffold', '200s as a no-op') not yet wired to revenue. Nothing mitigates the disconnection; the no-secret guard only mitigates the no-op case. Plan Phase 0 elaboration covers only systemCore/Safety/auth; Stripe/billing appears in no phase, so unaddressed is correct.
refined: The Stripe webhook is a not-yet-live scaffold that (a) skips signature verification (constructEvent is a TODO, JSON.parse of an unauthenticated body) and (b) writes entitlement to the in-memory ConnectionsStore via getStore().setLicense, which no tier-resolution path reads — so real Stripe payments won't grant Pro and forged events flip a value nothing reads; both are latent must-fix-before-billing correctness bugs, but neither is currently exploitable (no-op without the secret, inert write with it), making it low rather than a live spoofable-Pro vulnerability.

title: Inbound webhooks lack cryptographic signatures; the shared secret is optional and travels in the query string
mechanism: /api/webhooks/unipile and /api/connect/notify verify only an OPTIONAL shared secret (OSMO_WEBHOOK_SECRET); if unset, both are fully open. connect/notify additionally accepts the secret via ?secret= in the URL (set on notify_url), so it is logged by Unipile, Render, and proxies. With the secret unset or leaked, an attacker who learns/guesses a connection id can inject fabricated inbound messages into a victim device's oplog (published to its SSE + pulled as real messages) or force a connection to 'degraded'. OAuth-backed connection ids (gmail-/x-<deviceId suffix>) are partially derived from the deviceId, narrowing the guess.
breaksAt: Any deploy where OSMO_WEBHOOK_SECRET (sync:false) is not set, or once a notify URL with ?secret= is captured from logs.
evidence: web/app/api/webhooks/unipile/route.ts:10-13 (optional secret, no HMAC); web/app/api/connect/notify/route.ts:15-20 (?secret= query); web/lib/unipile/real.ts:50-52 (secret embedded in notify_url query)
reasoning: Code confirms the mechanism. web/app/api/webhooks/unipile/route.ts:10-13 gates on `if (secret && header !== secret)`, so when OSMO_WEBHOOK_SECRET is unset the endpoint is fully open; no HMAC/signature exists (grep for createHmac/signature hits only Stripe scaffold and license signing, never these routes). render.yaml:61-62 marks the secret sync:false, so unset-by-default is a realistic deploy state. The injection path is real: normalizeMessageWebhook (normalize.ts:155-187) requires only attacker-supplied account_type/chat_id/message_id/text, then store.appendRows(connection.deviceId, bundle) + publish(sync.dirty) inject a fabricated inbound message into the victim oplog/SSE (route.ts:25-31); account.status events flip a connection to degraded (route.ts:32-39). Preconditions match: store.connectionById(accountId) must resolve. OAuth ids are gmail-/x-/slack-${deviceId.slice(-8)} (oauth/*/callback/route.ts) — 32 bits, derivable from a known deviceId, as claimed. connect/notify accepts ?secret= (route.ts:17) and real.ts:50-52 embeds it in notify_url, so it is logged by Unipile/Render/proxies. Comparisons use non-constant-time !== (minor). Two factors keep this at medium rather than higher: both the not-found (route.ts:21) and success paths return 200 {ok:true}, giving a blind attacker no oracle to confirm a guessed id; and the notify endpoint's injection also requires a single-use link-<uuid> (122-bit, infeasible), so notify's real exposure is the query-string secret leak, not injection. Impact is in-memory, per-device fabricated-message/prompt-injection and connection DoS gated on knowing/guessing a connection id — real but bounded. Not mitigated. Plan Phase 0 elaboration covers only the /api/suggest systemCore/Safety/auth MUST-FIX, not webhook auth, so unaddressed.
refined: /api/webhooks/unipile performs no signature check and its shared secret is optional (OSMO_WEBHOOK_SECRET is sync:false, so unset by default), letting anyone who knows or guesses a connection id (OAuth ids are only 32 bits, derivable from the deviceId) inject fabricated inbound messages into a victim device's oplog/SSE or force a connection to 'degraded'; the connect/notify secret additionally rides in the URL query where logs capture it.

title: Send blocks on the full provider round-trip with no optimistic local echo and no server-side fetch timeout
