mechanism: POST /api/sync/send awaits the provider send (Gmail/Slack/X/Unipile) and only records the oplog row AFTER the provider returns the real platformMessageID (appendRows on line 62 is after the awaited send on lines 35-43). This is a deliberate 'no temp IDs' design (route header lines 1-4). So the drafted reply the user just tapped Send on does not appear anywhere until the entire upstream round-trip completes. The server-side fetch() calls in send.ts have NO timeout, so a TCP-connected-but-silent provider hangs the request indefinitely; the client (BackendClient.urlSessionTransport uses URLSession.shared.data, default 60s request timeout) gives up at 60s and throws, but the send may have actually succeeded upstream. Gmail is doubly slow: sendGmail makes TWO sequential upstream calls (thread-metadata GET on line 47, then messages/send POST on line 74) inside the one blocking request.
breaksAt: Any provider p95 spike; guaranteed felt on Gmail (2 serial round-trips). A hung provider hangs the request up to the client's 60s ceiling with no local echo the whole time.
reasoning: Mechanism verified end-to-end. web/app/api/sync/send/route.ts awaits the provider send at lines 35-43 and only calls store.appendRows at line 62 (after the send); header lines 1-4 confirm the deliberate no-temp-IDs, block-until-real-messageID design. web/lib/oauth/send.ts sendGmail issues two serial fetches (thread metadata GET line 47, messages/send POST line 74) and none of the provider fetches (Gmail/Slack/X, plus web/lib/unipile/real.ts call() line 21) carry any AbortSignal/timeout — server-side there is no application-level timeout. BackendClient.swift send() (119-126) routes through urlSessionTransport (345-346) using URLSession.shared.data with the default ~60s request timeout — notably the SSE path sets 90s explicitly (245/357) while the send path does not. App-side there is no optimistic echo for backend platforms: AppModel.send (973-978) awaits backend.send then ingests+reloads, and callers clear the draft only on success (SuggestionStrip.swift:160, InboxView.swift:818); iMessage uses a separate local path but Gmail/Slack/X/Unipile show nothing until the round-trip returns. On a client timeout the error falls to the general catch (982-995) → enqueueSend, and drainSendQueue re-sends, creating a duplicate-send risk if the original eventually succeeded upstream. Existing code partially mitigates adjacent concerns (offline queue with 5-attempt cap, 4xx surfaced as reject, re-pull dedup of the same oplog row) but does NOT provide optimistic echo or a server-side timeout, so the specific weakness stands. Not addressed by the plan (Phase 0 elaboration is systemCore/Safety/auth only; S0-S6 concern drafting, not send transport). Severity lowered from high to medium: this is a UX-latency + missing-timeout robustness defect with a conditional duplicate-send edge case, not a data-loss/security/availability breaker — sends normally complete quickly, loss is avoided by the offline queue, and impact is felt only under provider slowness (guaranteed only on Gmail's two serial calls) in a drafting assistant rather than a primary realtime messenger.
refined: POST /api/sync/send blocks on the full, un-timed provider round-trip before recording the oplog row (route.ts:35-62), and the app has no optimistic local echo for backend platforms (AppModel.swift:973-978), so a slow provider (worst on Gmail's two serial fetches) makes Send feel hung up to the client's ~60s URLSession default, after which the send is queued and can be re-sent as a duplicate if it actually succeeded upstream.

title: The crown-jewel interactive draft is fully blocking and non-streaming — user stares at a spinner for the whole generation with zero feedback
mechanism: /api/suggest calls Anthropic with a plain (non-streaming) fetch and returns only after the full completion is decoded (route.ts lines 88-108, max_tokens 700, no stream:true). The Swift client (ClaudeProxyGenerator) posts via URLSession.shared.data with NO custom timeout, so the interactive suggest call blocks up to URLSession's 60s default with no partial tokens, no progress, no first-token latency win. Prompt caching cuts cost but not wall-clock for generated output tokens; because the psychology core is a large cached system block, latency is dominated by output token generation that the user experiences as one long silent wait.
breaksAt: Felt on every call; painful whenever Sonnet output latency is multi-second, and any tail-latency event blocks with no feedback up to 60s.
reasoning: Mechanism verified in code. route.ts:78-86 builds anthropicBody with max_tokens:700 and NO stream:true; the fetch at :88-96 is plain, :101 does await res.json() (full decode), and :108 returns the entire completion as one JSON blob — server is fully blocking/non-streaming. ClaudeProxyGenerator.swift:33-37 uses URLSession.shared.data(for:) with no URLSessionConfiguration and no request.timeoutInterval, so it inherits the 60s default timeoutIntervalForRequest; :56-62 awaits the full body then decodes ProxyResponse — client blocks for the whole generation with no partial tokens. So the claim is factually correct on every point. However, severity 'high' is overstated: the interactive output is a small structured DraftSet capped at 700 tokens (few-second typical latency, not catastrophic), streaming raw tokens of a multi-take structured response has limited UX value, prompt caching does reduce TTFT (not only cost, as claimed), and there is no security/correctness/data-loss impact — this is UX/latency polish, i.e. medium. The 60s no-feedback tail is real but is a timeout-tuning concern layered on top. Plan S2 deliberately specifies SINGLE-SHOT interactive and Phase 4 addresses only the background path, so streaming/first-token feedback is genuinely unaddressed and not mitigated anywhere.
refined: The interactive draft is returned as a single non-streaming JSON blob (route.ts:88-108, no stream:true, await res.json()) and the client blocks on URLSession.shared.data with the default 60s timeout and no partial feedback (ClaudeProxyGenerator.swift:33-37,56), so every primary draft is a blocking spinner with no first-token win — a real UX/tail-latency polish item (medium), not a high-severity defect given the modest 700-token structured output.

title: No retries on the LLM wire, and the client falls back to mock ONLY on URLError — a transient Anthropic 529/overload becomes a hard user-facing failure
mechanism: The proxy does zero retries and maps any non-2xx Anthropic response to a 502 (suggest/route.ts lines 98-100). The Swift ClaudeProxyGenerator throws GenerationError.http(502) for non-2xx (line 57). GeneratorRouter.generate catches ONLY GenerationError.notConfigured and URLError (ClaudeProxyGenerator.swift lines 82-84) — a GenerationError.http(502) is NOT caught, so it propagates as a hard failure to SuggestionService.suggest. So Anthropic's common transient 429/529 (overloaded) turns straight into a failed draft with no retry and no mock fallback, even though the router was designed to degrade gracefully. All 7 call sites (suggest/judge/threadIntel/insight/dossier/ask/voicePersona) share this one wire and inherit the gap.
breaksAt: Manifests under Anthropic load spikes (529s are routine at scale) and any brief upstream blip — no retry means single-shot failure.
reasoning: Mechanism verified end-to-end and is real. web/app/api/suggest/route.ts:88-100 does a single fetch to Anthropic with zero retries and collapses ANY non-2xx (429/503/529/500) to a 502. ClaudeProxyGenerator.swift:57 turns that into GenerationError.http(502). GeneratorRouter.generate (ClaudeProxyGenerator.swift:79-86) catches ONLY GenerationError.notConfigured and URLError, so http(502) bypasses the mock fallback and propagates. SuggestionService.suggest (SuggestionService.swift:28-32) does not catch it, and the interactive UI (SuggestionStrip.swift:144-146) shows a hard "Couldn't draft — try again." with no auto-retry. All 7 call sites share this wire (generator.generate in MessageJudge/Insight/ThreadIntel/Dossier/Ask/VoiceStats + suggest). Not mitigated: the only retry/backoff in the repo is BackendClient.swift (sync/SSE 401+SSE backoff) and web/lib/enrich/exa.ts — neither touches the LLM wire. Two reasons to downgrade from high to medium: (1) framing is off — the mock fallback is explicitly for keyless/dev/unreachable, and serving [mock] placeholder text to a paid user during a blip would be worse than the honest error, so the real remedy is bounded retry/backoff on retryable statuses, not mock fallback; (2) blast radius is one user-recoverable failed draft with an honest message; background paths use try? (AppModel.swift:870) and silently skip or use deterministic fallbacks (Insight/Dossier/ThreadIntel/VoiceStats have static fallback()). No data loss, security, or correctness impact. Plan is partial: Phase 4 names cold-start/outage handling (where wire retry/backoff would live) but does not specify retry/backoff for the interactive single-shot S2 path, and Phase 0 elaboration only covers systemCore/Safety/auth.
refined: Neither the proxy nor the Swift client retries retryable upstream errors — the proxy collapses Anthropic 429/503/529 to a 502 with no backoff, and GeneratorRouter catches only URLError/notConfigured, so a transient overload propagates as GenerationError.http and surfaces as a hard "Couldn't draft — try again" on the interactive path (medium: user-recoverable, no data/security impact; fix is bounded retry/backoff on retryable statuses, not mock fallback).

title: Backfills are fire-and-forget promises with no durable queue/resume; the 'reconciliation poller' that supposedly retries them does not exist server-side
mechanism: Every backfill is launched as an un-awaited `void backfill*(...)` inside a request process (connect/notify:47, rebackfill:26, accounts:48 on resume, and all 3 oauth callbacks). backfill.ts:22 and :153 claim 'the reconciliation poller retries', but grep shows NO server-side timer ever re-invokes backfillConnection — the only 'reconciliation timer' is the CLIENT's cursor-pull (events.ts:3-4), which just re-pulls existing rows and cannot resume a dead import. So a redeploy/crash/promise-rejection mid-import kills the in-flight backfill and leaves the connection pinned 'backfilling' or 'degraded' forever; the user waits indefinitely for history that never lands until they manually hit /api/connect/rebackfill. Since backfill state and the oplog are in-memory on one instance, a Render redeploy wipes both mid-import.
breaksAt: Every deploy that overlaps an in-flight backfill; guaranteed on first-connect for large accounts because the import runs tens of seconds.
reasoning: Mechanism verified. All backfills are un-awaited fire-and-forget: notify/route.ts:47, rebackfill/route.ts:26, accounts/route.ts:48 (void backfillConnection), plus the 3 OAuth callbacks (google:48 backfillGmail, x:49 backfillX, slack:53 backfillSlack). No durable queue or resume exists. Critically, the comments at backfill.ts:22 and :153 claiming a "reconciliation poller retries" are false: grep shows the only server-side timers are the SSE heartbeat (events.ts:56), the mock drip (mock.ts:68), and an enrichment retry (exa.ts:31) — none re-invoke any backfill. The "reconciliation timer" referenced in events.ts:3-4/types.ts:136 is the CLIENT cursor pull; sync/pull/route.ts:20 is a pure oplog read that re-delivers existing rows and cannot resume a dead import. The only restart-heal (adoptOrphanedAccounts, accounts/route.ts:24) is gated OSMO_ADOPT_ORPHANS=1, off by default, re-runs a full backfill (not a resume), and its own comment says enabling it in multi-tenant prod is wrong — so no mitigation in production. Two accuracy issues drop this from high to medium: (1) the "pinned backfilling/degraded forever" outcome only holds for the in-process caught-error path (backfill.ts:155 -> degraded, no retry); on an actual redeploy/crash the in-memory store is wiped entirely, so the connection record and all oplog rows vanish (connection gone, not pinned) — that total loss is really the known in-memory-durability issue, of which backfill non-resumability is a subset. (2) It is recoverable (provider is source of truth; user can hit rebackfill), non-security, no silent corruption. planStatus partial: Phase 0's title names "durable state" but its elaboration only specifies the systemCore/Safety/auth fix, and Phase 4 mentions cold-start/outage only loosely — no concrete backfill-queue/resume step.
refined: Backfills are non-resumable fire-and-forget promises with no durable queue and no server-side retry poller (the "reconciliation poller" in backfill.ts:22/:153 is fictional — the only reconciliation timer is the client's read-only cursor pull), so a caught mid-import error leaves the connection stuck "degraded" with no auto-recovery, and a redeploy/crash wipes the in-memory store (connection + all imported oplog rows) with re-adoption disabled by default; real reliability gap, but medium because recoverable and largely a facet of the broader in-memory-durability problem.

title: Gmail backfill does up to 300 SEQUENTIAL full-message fetches and appends to the oplog ONCE at the very end — nothing lands for the whole import and a restart loses all of it
mechanism: gmailBackfill accumulates every message/contact/thread in local Maps and calls store.appendRows/publish exactly ONCE after the loop (lines ~232-238), unlike the Unipile backfill which appends per page (backfill.ts:120). The loop does one sequential `messages/{id}?format=full` fetch per message up to MAX_MESSAGES=300 (gmailBackfill.ts:13,182-183), each a serial round-trip. So the user sees zero messages and gets zero sync.dirty doorbells for the entire import, then everything appears at once — and if the single instance restarts or the promise throws before the final append, the whole import is discarded with nothing persisted and no resume. 300 serial fetches also monopolize the single instance's outbound for the duration.
breaksAt: Every Gmail import of non-trivial size; total loss on any restart within the multi-second window (which for Gmail is the whole import).
reasoning: Core mechanism holds against the code. gmailBackfill.ts:183-224 accumulates every contact/thread/message into local Map/array and calls store.appendRows exactly once at 232-235 with a single sync.dirty publish at 235 (plus one empty-append doorbell in finish(), 244-249). The per-message loop at 189 does one serial, awaited `messages/{id}?format=full` fetch each, up to MAX_MESSAGES=300 (13) / PAGE_SIZE=100 (12). No progress/backfill.progress or sync.dirty is emitted during the loop. This genuinely contrasts with Unipile (backfill.ts:120 appends per page, 140-141 publishes backfill.progress + sync.dirty per page), which — because the app cursor-pulls each doorbell into its durable on-Mac store — gives Unipile incremental durability that Gmail lacks; Gmail's all-or-nothing window equals the entire (serial-fetch) import. On throw, catch at 237-240 sets degraded and nothing is appended. On restart, oplog + connection + OAuth token are all in-memory and wiped, and there is NO resume for Gmail: adoptOrphanedAccounts (accounts/route.ts:24-53) is off by default (OSMO_ADOPT_ORPHANS!=="1") and even when on only re-adopts Unipile accounts, not OAuth platforms. So the loss-with-no-auto-resume is real. Two things temper the claimed HIGH: (1) the loss is NOT permanent — appendRows is idempotent (memoryStore.ts:214 dedups on platform:platformMessageID→contentHash), so a manual reconnect+re-backfill cleanly re-imports recent (60d/15d) history; (2) "300 serial fetches monopolize the single instance's outbound" is overstated — `await fetch` is non-blocking async I/O, so unrelated requests are still served during the awaits; it only serializes this one backfill (slow import), not the instance. Also note the single-end-append pattern is not unique to Gmail — xBackfill.ts:101-104 does the same; Gmail is uniquely bad only because its per-message full fetches make the window the longest of any platform. Net: a real robustness/UX gap (zero incremental durability, zero progress feedback, no Gmail reconciliation) but recoverable, low-probability trigger, and no security/permanent-loss impact → medium, not high. planStatus partial is fair: Phase 0's "durable state" theme would cover the restart-loss root cause but the elaboration explicitly specifies only the systemCore/Safety/auth MUST-FIX, and no phase addresses per-page append granularity or backfill progress.
refined: Gmail backfill's up-to-300 serial per-message full fetches (gmailBackfill.ts:189) feed a single end-of-loop appendRows/doorbell (232-235, like X, unlike per-page Unipile), so the import has no incremental durability, no progress feedback, and no Gmail reconciliation/resume — any throw or single-instance restart in that (longest-of-any-platform) window delivers zero rows, recoverable only by a manual reconnect + idempotent re-backfill; real but medium (recoverable, low-probability, and it does not actually block the event loop).

title: Background LLM traffic (threadIntel per inbound + up to 30 autodrafts/day) shares the one blocking wire, Anthropic account, and single instance with the interactive draft — no priority lane
mechanism: threadIntel (one full Sonnet call per thread per new inbound, ThreadIntel.swift:167-173) and autodraft precompute (up to dailyCap=30/day, AutodraftPolicy.swift:16) all go through the same GeneratorRouter -> /api/suggest wire as the user's interactive draft. There is no separate endpoint, model tier, queue, or rate budget separating background from interactive traffic, and Render pins ONE instance. A burst of inbound (backfill completion firing many threadIntels, or a morning email dump) fires N concurrent full-model calls that contend with the interactive draft the user is actively waiting on for the shared Anthropic account concurrency/rate limit — the interactive call the user blocks on can queue behind background work or trip a shared 429.
breaksAt: Manifests once inbound volume x threadIntel + autodraft approaches the Anthropic account's per-minute rate/concurrency limit; a single active user post-backfill can trigger it.
reasoning: Mechanism holds: interactive drafts (SuggestionStrip.swift:136), autodrafts (AppModel.swift:870, cap 30/day), and threadIntel (AppModel.swift:1122 -> ThreadIntel.swift:169) all funnel through SuggestionService.generator.generate -> GeneratorRouter (ClaudeProxyGenerator.swift:79-87) -> the single /api/suggest route (confirmed the only LLM endpoint). All use the same config.model ('claude-sonnet-5'); the request carries no background/priority flag and `count` is ignored server-side. There is no separate endpoint, queue, model tier, or rate budget. Crucially, /api/suggest applies NO rate limit to Pro (quota gate route.ts:63-76 only bites free tier), and background traffic is Pro-gated (ensureIntel/autodraft both require isPro) — so the users generating background load have zero server-side concurrency cap, while /api/enrich/person DOES implement a per-device 10/min window, showing the pattern exists but is unapplied here. Under burst, Anthropic 429 surfaces to the user's interactive draft as a 502 (route.ts:98-99). However the claimed severity (high) overstates: this is intermittent reliability contention (not security/data-loss), depends on Anthropic account tier + coincident use, and is materially bounded by existing code — importInChunks (RealtimeSyncEngine.swift:382-406) discards `fresh` so the LARGE first backfill does NOT fan out autodrafts (defusing the 'post-backfill fan-out' trigger), the freshWindow gate (line 232) excludes historical messages, and per-thread dedup + 30/day cap bound concurrency. The 'one blocking wire/single instance' framing is imprecise since Node multiplexes; the real shared choke is the single Anthropic key's account rate/concurrency limit. The morning-inbound-burst and inbox threadIntel fan-out triggers remain unmitigated. Plan is partial: it conceptually separates S2 single-shot interactive from BACKGROUND-ONLY S4/S5 (and a cheaper Haiku judge) with Phase 4 covering background quality/outage, but never specifies a priority queue, separate rate budget, or concurrency isolation between the lanes on the shared endpoint/key.
refined: All LLM traffic — the interactive draft plus background threadIntel and up to 30 autodrafts/day — shares one unprioritized /api/suggest endpoint on a single Anthropic key with no rate/concurrency budget or priority separating background from interactive (and no Pro-tier rate cap at all), so a morning inbound burst or inbox threadIntel fan-out can trip a shared 429 that the route surfaces to the user's interactive draft as a 502; real but medium, since it is intermittent, tier/load-dependent, and already bounded by dedup, daily caps, the freshWindow gate, and importInChunks defusing the large-backfill fan-out.

title: Enrichment blocks the user on two paid upstreams fetched sequentially with no cache; repeated lookups re-fetch and hit a per-process 10/60s wall
mechanism: enrichPerson awaits Unipile getUserProfile FIRST (person.ts:42) then Exa searchPerson (line 55) — sequential, not Promise.all — so the dossier open blocks on the sum of both round-trips, with no server-side fetch timeout. There is no result cache anywhere in web/lib/enrich, so flipping back to the same person re-fetches both paid upstreams every time; the only guard is a per-process sliding window of 10/60s (enrich/person/route.ts:12-23) which, once tripped, 429s the user's dossier open. On a restart the window map is wiped (in-memory), and on the single instance the window is global to the process.
breaksAt: Every cold dossier open pays serial upstream latency; the 11th lookup in 60s hard-fails with 429.
reasoning: Code-confirmed facts, but the framing is substantially wrong. (1) SEQUENTIAL is real (person.ts:42 profile then :55 exa) but it is a deliberate data dependency: person.ts:51-53 builds the Exa query hints from profile.company/profile.title ("The profile sharpens the query for common names"), so Promise.all would degrade results — this is intentional, not a missed parallelization. (2) NO SERVER FETCH TIMEOUT is real: neither RealUnipileClient.call (real.ts:20-35) nor exaPost (exa.ts) uses AbortController/AbortSignal, and exaPost retries up to 3x with backoff, which can compound latency. (3) NO SERVER-SIDE CACHE is real in web/lib/enrich, but the "re-fetches both paid upstreams every time you flip back" premise is FALSE: the Mac app persists enrichment on-device in SQLite (PersonEnrichment.swift, person_enrichment table) and AppModel.ensureEnrichment (AppModel.swift:1290) returns without calling the backend when a non-stale (<7-day) copy exists, plus in-flight (:1291) and empty-this-session (:1292) guards. The durable cache is device-local by design (local-first app; route.ts comment: "holds no state beyond a per-device rate window"). (4) "Blocks the user" is FALSE: ensureEnrichment runs in a detached Task (AppModel.swift:1306) and PeopleView.profileCard renders immediately from cached state; the fetch is background, not on the render path. (5) The 10/60s window (route.ts:12-23) is keyed per-device, not "global to the process," and a 429 does NOT hard-fail the dossier — AppModel.swift:1311-1315 keeps cached data and stays silent unless it was a forced refresh. So the residual is only: no server-side deadline on a background enrichment call and a low per-device burst cap that bites only a first-run scan of 11+ genuinely-new contacts within a minute, degrading gracefully. That is low severity, not medium. Plan Phase 5 names an "enrichment cache" (covers the caching angle at a high level) but does not address the missing timeout or the burst-cap 429 and is deferred to a late phase, so planStatus is partial.
refined: The enrichment route has no server-side fetch timeout and fetches LinkedIn-then-Exa sequentially (an intentional dependency: the Exa query is sharpened by the profile's company/title), so a genuinely cold, uncached person's BACKGROUND enrichment can pay summed, deadline-less upstream latency — but return visits never re-fetch (7-day on-device SQLite cache plus in-flight/empty guards), the fetch runs off the render path so the dossier is never blocked, and 429s degrade silently, making this a low-severity robustness gap rather than a medium blocking/cost failure.

title: Media proxy fully buffers Gmail/Unipile attachments (no streaming) and enforces the 25MB cap AFTER allocating the buffer — double latency plus OOM risk on the one instance
mechanism: For Gmail the route does res.json() to pull the entire base64 payload into memory, Buffer.from(...) decodes the whole file, and only then binaryResponse checks byteLength > MAX_BYTES (media/route.ts:64-66,29-30) — so an over-cap file is fully downloaded and decoded before rejection. The Unipile path also fully buffers (downloadAttachment returns bytes, line 100). Only Slack streams via res.body passthrough (lines 90-95). The user therefore waits for the full server-side download + decode + re-upload (serialized, not streamed) for Gmail/Unipile media, and concurrent large-attachment requests each hold the whole file in RAM on the single starter instance, risking OOM.
breaksAt: Latency felt on any sizeable Gmail/Unipile attachment; memory pressure/OOM when a few near-cap fetches overlap on one instance.
reasoning: Code confirms the core mechanism. Gmail path (media/route.ts:59-66) does await res.json() to pull the full base64 body, Buffer.from(...,'base64url') to decode the whole file, and only binaryResponse (line 30) checks byteLength>MAX_BYTES — so an over-cap file is fully downloaded and decoded before the 413, with no early content-length pre-check. Unipile path (lib/unipile/real.ts:195-202) does Buffer.from(await res.arrayBuffer()), fully buffering with no size check and no streaming, even though it is a binary endpoint that could have streamed res.body + checked content-length like Slack. Only the Slack path (route.ts:88-95) checks content-length before streaming res.body, so the claim's contrast is accurate; the Gmail/Unipile paths are genuinely NOT mitigated. However, severity of 'medium' is a bit generous: the route is authenticated (requireDevice, line 41) and user-initiated (opening one's own attachments), per-request memory is bounded (25MB cap; ~116MB worst-case peak for a maximally over-cap Gmail file incl. the base64 JSON copy), and cache-control private max-age=86400 curbs repeat fetches. Crucially the 'no streaming/double latency' critique is largely inherent for Gmail, whose attachment API only returns base64 in a JSON body — buffering is unavoidable there, so the honest Gmail fix is an early content-length pre-check, not streaming. The OOM concern is real and amplified because OOM on the single 512MB stateful instance wipes all in-memory state (oplog/tokens/quota/SSE), but it requires atypical concurrent large fetches across multiple devices, which is low likelihood for a personal relationship app on a starter plan. Plan Phase 0 elaboration only covers the systemCore/Safety/auth MUST-FIX; media buffering is not mentioned anywhere, so unaddressed.
refined: Gmail (route.ts:64-66) and Unipile (real.ts:202) fully buffer — and for Gmail base64-decode — the entire attachment into RAM before the 25MB cap is checked, so over-cap files are downloaded/decoded before the 413 and concurrent large fetches transiently spike memory on the single 512MB instance; real but low-severity since it is authenticated and user-initiated, per-request memory is bounded, Gmail's JSON-base64 API makes buffering inherent, and the cheap fix is an early content-length check (plus streaming res.body for the binary Unipile path like Slack).

title: LLM message-derivatives (dossiers/insights/voice) + attachment bytes are stored PLAINTEXT outside SQLCipher and SURVIVE "Erase all data"
mechanism: The whole privacy story is 'encrypted on your Mac' (SQLCipher DB keyed by KeychainDBKey). But the app writes a fleet of message-DERIVED artifacts as plaintext JSON in Application Support, bypassing the encrypted store entirely: dossiers.json (AppModel.swift:1429-1438), insights.json (AppModel.swift:1442-1451), voicePersona.json (AppModel.swift:1521-1531), plus account.json/connections.json/cursors.json/autodraftCap.json; and downloaded attachment bytes land in a plaintext dir at Application Support/Osmo/media (MediaStore.swift:16-20). deleteAllData() (AppModel.swift:1844-1849) only calls store.deleteAllData() (DB rows, OsmoStore+Shell.swift:240-254), clears the device token, and unsets hasOnboarded. It removes NONE of those files and never calls a MediaStore clear. So the distilled relationship intelligence (arguably more sensitive than raw rows) and the attachment media remain readable on disk after the user hits ERASE, after account deletion, and after a Mac is sold/returned. exportJSON() (OsmoStore+Shell.swift:210-235) also omits dossiers/insights/voicePersona/media, so 'Export all data' is simultaneously INCOMPLETE.
breaksAt: Manifests for essentially every engaged user; realized as a privacy/compliance breach on device resale, forensic access, or a GDPR/CCPA erasure/portability request.
evidence: App/Sources/App/AppModel.swift:1429-1531 (dossiers/insights/voicePersona plaintext writes), MediaStore.swift:16-20, AppModel.swift:1844-1849 (deleteAllData) + OsmoStore+Shell.swift:240-254 (exportJSON/deleteAllData scope)
reasoning: Verified every cited line. The DB is genuinely SQLCipher AES-encrypted (OsmoDatabase.swift:4-11, "what backs the 'encrypted on your Mac' guarantee"), so plaintext derivatives are a real contrast. dossiers.json (AppModel.swift:1436-1439), insights.json (1449-1452), and voicePersona.json (1528-1531) are written as raw JSONEncoder output to Application Support/Osmo (supportDir, 1757-1764) with no encryption; these are LLM-distilled per-person relationship narratives and a voice persona derived from the user's own sent messages. Attachment full-size bytes are written plaintext to Application Support/Osmo/media (MediaStore.swift:16-40); MediaStore has NO clear/delete method at all. deleteAllData() (AppModel.swift:1844-1850) only wipes DB rows (store.deleteAllData, OsmoStore+Shell.swift:240-254), clears the device token, and unsets hasOnboarded, then toasts "All local data erased" — it removes none of the JSON caches and never clears the media dir (the only removeItem calls in AppModel are at :331 entitlement refresh and :1752 DB-corruption recovery, neither in the erase path). deleteAccount() (:389-401) routes through the same incomplete deleteAllData(). exportJSON() (OsmoStore+Shell.swift:210-235) exports only DB tables and omits dossiers/insights/voicePersona/media, so export is also incomplete. No existing code mitigates. Not addressed by any plan phase (Phase 0 elaboration is only the systemCore/Safety/auth proxy fix). Severity kept high: contradicts a core marketed guarantee and the in-app erase toast, though it requires local disk/backup/forensic access rather than a remote exploit.
refined: "Erase all data"/"Delete account" (AppModel.swift:1844-1850, 389-401) wipes only SQLCipher DB rows and the device token while leaving LLM-derived relationship artifacts (dossiers.json/insights.json/voicePersona.json) and the entire plaintext Application Support/Osmo/media attachment-bytes directory (MediaStore has no delete method) intact and readable, and exportJSON omits the same set — so the "encrypted on your Mac / all local data erased" guarantee is false for every message-derived cache.

title: Voice/dossier/insight calls egress VERBATIM private sent-messages to Claude; the 'keep none / never train' proof cannot cover Anthropic-side retention and the model is client-chosen
mechanism: PERSONALIZATION isn't just 'pseudonymous scalars': VoicePersona.compose ships up to 20 verbatim user sent-messages as 'SAMPLE LINES' (VoiceStats.swift:156-159) and ensureVoicePersona feeds outboundMessages(limit:60) — 60 raw sent messages — into the proxy (AppModel.swift:1506-1510). The proxy forwards them to api.anthropic.com with NO retention control: no ZDR/no-train header, no org/DPA assertion, and the MODEL is taken from client body.model unvalidated (suggest/route.ts:46,78-96). The stated privacy proof is a 'log-scrubbing test' — but that can only prove the PROXY stores nothing; it is structurally incapable of proving Anthropic's upstream 30-day abuse-retention isn't holding these messages, and a client passing an arbitrary model string sidesteps any model-scoped ZDR the org might have. The claim 'we keep none, never train' is thus asserted about a third party the code neither configures nor can enforce.
breaksAt: Live from the first keyed request; becomes a broken-promise/legal exposure the instant a user, auditor, or regulator asks to see the ZDR+DPA behind the marketing sentence.
reasoning: Code confirms the egress: VoiceStats.swift:156-159 ships up to 20 verbatim sent-messages as SAMPLE LINES, and Dossier.swift:96 / ThreadIntel.swift:77 / Insight.swift:52 / PromptComposer.swift:158 / MessageJudge.swift:58 ship raw 'You:/Them:' thread text; AppModel.swift:1506 pulls outboundMessages(limit:60) (though only 20 reach the prompt via suffix(20), so '60 into the proxy' is slightly overstated). suggest/route.ts:46 takes model = body.model with NO allowlist (grep found zero model validation), ClaudeProxyGenerator.swift:49 sends the runtime-overridable config.model, and route.ts:88-96 sets only x-api-key/anthropic-version/content-type — no retention control; grep found no ZDR/DPA/no-train anywhere except the marketing string 'zero-retention terms' at privacy/page.tsx:34. So the mechanism is REAL and unmitigated. But the framing over-reaches: (1) 'never train' is Anthropic's default commercial term for API data, not an unenforceable promise; (2) ZDR is an org/account-level agreement, not a per-request header, so 'no ZDR/no-train header' conflates the two — there is no such header to send; (3) 'model-scoped ZDR sidestep' is speculative since ZDR is org-wide; (4) the egress itself is disclosed ('to draft we send Claude the thread+notes+goal'), so it is not a hidden leak. The genuinely code-actionable defects are the unpinned/unvalidated model (route.ts:46 — a cost/abuse and routing-outside-arrangement risk) and the affirmative 'zero-retention'/'keep none' representation resting on an unverified upstream Anthropic ZDR/DPA that the proxy-side log-scrubbing 'stores nothing' proof structurally cannot cover. That is a real contractual/disclosure exposure but not a code-exploitable data path beyond the disclosed egress, hence severity medium, not high. Plan Phase 0 elaboration only specifies the systemCore/Safety/auth fix (MUST-FIX list omits model pinning and ZDR/DPA), so this specific issue is only partially addressed by the plan's broader privacy promise.
refined: The proxy ships genuinely sensitive verbatim private sent-messages (VoiceStats.swift:156-159; Dossier/ThreadIntel/Insight/PromptComposer 'You:/Them:' text) to api.anthropic.com with a client-chosen, unvalidated model (suggest/route.ts:46) and no in-code retention control, so the site's 'zero-retention terms'/'keep none' representation (privacy/page.tsx:34) rests entirely on an unverified org-level Anthropic ZDR/DPA that a proxy-side log-scrubbing test cannot prove — though 'never train' is already Anthropic's default commercial term and ZDR is an account-level agreement, not a per-request header, making the true fix model-pinning plus a signed ZDR/DPA rather than any code header.

title: Signed config registry introduces a THIRD app-bundled Ed25519 key whose compromise is a silent fleet-wide push — and which itself can only be rotated by the OTA it exists to avoid
mechanism: The system already has two long-lived Ed25519 domains with no rotation story: the entitlement key (public half hard-baked in the binary, EntitlementVerifier.swift:34; payload field 'v' is a schema version, NOT a key id; committed DEV private fallback in sign.ts:15-18) and the Sparkle EdDSA key (Info.plist:39). The plan adds a THIRD: a signed config registry carrying directives/thresholds/PER-TASK MODEL IDS/kill-switch flags (today the flags route is entirely UNSIGNED, flags/route.ts). Its verifying public key must be byte-stable and app-bundled, so the one key that can push new prompts, swap model ids (cost/safety), or flip aiDrafting for ALL users instantly — with NO Apple notarization/Gatekeeper in the loop that a real OTA has — cannot be rotated or revoked without shipping the very OTA the registry was built to eliminate. There is no kid, no rotation, no revocation, no expiry in any of the three domains. A leak of the config private key is a silent, un-recallable, fleet-wide control-plane takeover strictly worse than a bad OTA.
breaksAt: At the moment rotation is first needed, or on first key compromise; blast radius = entire installed base, applied without user consent or OS gatekeeping.
reasoning: Code checks out. EntitlementVerifier.swift:34 hard-bakes one prod Ed25519 public key; sign.ts:18 commits DEV_PRIVATE_D used as a fallback at line 39; the payload's 'v' is a schema version (entitlement.ts:32 sets v:1), not a kid — no key id/rotation/revocation/expiry exists in any domain (grep for kid|rotat|revoc across web/Sources/App is empty). Info.plist:39-40 confirms the Sparkle SUPublicEDKey is likewise app-bundled and unrotatable. flags/route.ts confirms today's kill-switch/flags route is entirely UNSIGNED. The plan (lines 21/23) does propose a THIRD Ed25519 domain — a signed registry carrying directives, per-task model ids, and flags, with a 'byte-stable, app-bundled' verifying key — and specifies signing but NOT any kid/rotation/revocation, so the specific weakness (unrotatable trust anchor) is left unaddressed while the surrounding security phase touches the area: partial is right. The mechanism is real: a leaked config-signing key is a fleet-wide, un-recallable push whose trust anchor can only be replaced by the very OTA the registry exists to avoid, and it bypasses the Gatekeeper/notarization a Sparkle OTA gets. Two framing corrections drop it from high to medium: (1) it is a design gap in an UNBUILT Phase-2 component, trivially fixable at design time by including a kid + bundled rotation/override anchor; (2) 'strictly worse than a bad OTA' is overstated — config (prompts/model ids/flags) cannot execute native code, unlike a Sparkle OTA which ships arbitrary signed binary, so blast radius is degraded behavior/cost/safety, not RCE. Also note the committed dev private key is not itself a live prod forgery vector because the bundled prod pubkey (lRI-...) does not match DEV_PUBLIC_X (4Y5M...), so a missing env fails closed to Free.
refined: The proposed Phase-2 signed config registry would add a third app-bundled Ed25519 trust anchor governing prompts, per-task model ids, and the kill-switch with no kid/rotation/revocation/expiry specified, making a leaked config-signing key a Gatekeeper-bypassing, fleet-wide control-plane liability revocable only via the OTA the registry exists to eliminate — a genuine key-management design gap, but config-only (no code execution) and in an unbuilt component, so medium rather than high.

title: Built-but-dormant E2EE blob sync flatly contradicts 'messages never leave your Mac', and its static-salt HKDF makes 'server can't read' false for the whole userbase the moment it's enabled
mechanism: SyncEngine + BlobStore ship a complete off-device oplog sync; the real store is 'a thin Supabase/S3 client added with credentials last' (BlobStore.swift:12-14). Enabling it sends the encrypted message oplog to Supabase/S3 — directly contradicting the headline claim AND the plan's 'only de-identified pseudonymous scalars leave device.' Worse, the encryption that supposedly saves the claim is broken as designed: CryptoBox.key() uses HKDF-SHA256 (NOT a memory-hard KDF) over a passphrase with a SINGLE hardcoded GLOBAL salt 'osmo.static.salt.replace.in.prod' (CryptoBox.swift:32-39). Static global salt ⇒ identical passphrase yields the identical key for EVERY user and enables one precomputation attack amortized across the entire userbase; HKDF (no Argon2id) makes offline passphrase brute-force cheap. So 'the server only ever stores opaque ciphertext it can never read' collapses to trivially-crackable the day sync is switched on.
breaksAt: First real deployment of sync; brute-force feasibility is worst for any low-entropy/shared passphrase and is population-wide because the salt is global.
reasoning: The cited code is literally accurate: a full E2EE oplog sync exists (SyncEngine.swift push/pull; OsmoStore+Sync.swift:7-8,21-39 exportChanges does SELECT * including the `message` table, so message bodies would ride the oplog as ciphertext), and CryptoBox.key uses HKDF-SHA256 with a static defaultSalt "osmo.static.salt.replace.in.prod" (CryptoBox.swift:32-39). But the severity/framing is off. (1) The entire blob-sync subsystem is DORMANT: grep for SyncEngine(/BlobStore/CryptoBox/exportChanges/.push() outside Sources/OsmoCore/Sync/ returns only definitions and Tests/OsmoCoreTests/SyncEngineTests.swift — no App/ wiring, and no real BlobStore exists (BlobStore.swift:13-14: the production Supabase/S3 client is "added with credentials last"). The app's live sync is a different class, RealtimeSyncEngine (Backend/), wired in AppModel.swift, which pulls from the backend + reads local chat.db and never invokes the encrypt-and-push path. (2) The weak KDF is explicitly self-documented as a placeholder to be swapped for Argon2id before production (CryptoBox.swift:9-12,31), and the salt constant literally reads "...replace.in.prod" (line 39). (3) The salt is a default PARAMETER (init at line 16 accepts a per-user salt), so "hardcoded global salt" only describes the default. (4) "Flatly contradicts 'messages never leave your Mac'" is overstated — E2EE ciphertext sync is a valid posture and the code scopes the claim to "(readably)" (lines 4-7). Exploitation requires three deliberate future steps against explicit in-code warnings (build a real BlobStore, wire SyncEngine in, ship without fixing the KDF/salt), so the current-state impact is a pre-ship design gate, not a population-wide break. Not already mitigated at the crypto level (HKDF+static salt is what runs if instantiated), but exploitability is fully contained by dormancy. Plan Phase 0 covers only systemCore/Safety/auth and does not mention this subsystem, so it is unaddressed by the plan (though the subsystem is arguably outside the plan's roadmap, which uses RealtimeSyncEngine, not blob sync).
refined: A complete but entirely dormant/test-only E2EE blob-sync (SyncEngine/BlobStore/CryptoBox, no production wiring and no real BlobStore) ships with a KDF that is explicitly flagged in-code as a placeholder (HKDF-SHA256 + a default "replace.in.prod" salt that can be overridden per-user); the genuine, low-severity action item is a pre-ship gate — do not productionize blob sync until the KDF is Argon2id with a per-user salt — not a live, population-wide "server can read everything" break.

title: SyncEngine push/pull cursors aren't persisted → every relaunch re-appends ALL local changes (unbounded duplicate-ciphertext growth) and re-decrypts the entire remote log
mechanism: SyncEngine holds lastPushedSeq/pullCursor in memory only, defaulting to 0, with the doc admitting 'the app persists them across launches (a small follow-up — noted, not blocking)' (SyncEngine.swift:5-21). Because the BlobStore is append-only, on every relaunch push() re-exports and re-appends the ENTIRE local change set as fresh ciphertext blobs (BlobStore has no dedup — BlobStore.swift:30-38), so the server log grows without bound proportional to launch count, and pull() with pullCursor=0 re-fetches, re-decrypts, and re-applies the whole log each launch. This is a storage-cost and battery/CPU sink that compounds silently, and it undermines any DR reasoning about the remote log size.
breaksAt: Grows linearly per relaunch per device; becomes a cost/performance problem for heavy users within days of enabling sync.
reasoning: Code-level claims are accurate: SyncEngine.swift:11-21 holds lastPushedSeq/pullCursor in memory defaulting to 0, lines 5-6 admit persistence is an unshipped follow-up, push() (line 26) calls exportChanges(sinceDeviceSeq: lastPushedSeq) which with 0 returns ALL local rows (OsmoStore+Sync.swift:21-39), and InMemoryBlobStore.append has no dedup (BlobStore.swift:30-38). So it is not a misread. BUT the impact framing is wrong. grep shows SyncEngine and InMemoryBlobStore are instantiated ONLY in Tests/OsmoCoreTests/SyncEngineTests.swift — never in the shipping app; and there is no real (Supabase/S3) BlobStore (the only Supabase store, web/lib/accounts/store.ts, holds accounts, not a blob log). The claim's own trigger concedes it needs 'a real BlobStore connected,' which is unbuilt. The live sync path is RealtimeSyncEngine (AppModel.swift:556-558) using a persistent FileCursorStore -> cursors.json (RealtimeSyncEngine.swift:12-36), which loads/saves both the backend cursor and the chat.db ROWID watermark, saves AFTER ingest for crash-safety (line 213), resets on 401 re-register (line 134), and re-pull is idempotent (INSERT OR REPLACE + LWW, OsmoStore+Sync.swift:50-79). So the claimed 'unbounded duplicate growth / re-decrypt entire log every launch / cost within days' cannot occur in the product; the production engine already persists cursors. This is a documented latent gap in an unwired E2EE scaffold, not an active weakness. Plan Phase 0 names 'durable state' generically but its elaboration only specifies systemCore/Safety/auth, so this specific persistence is not itemized — but it is de-facto handled on the live path.
refined: Real but low/latent: the E2EE SyncEngine's in-memory, default-0 cursors + dedup-free append are only exercised by SyncEngineTests (no real BlobStore exists), while the actual shipping sync engine (RealtimeSyncEngine + FileCursorStore/cursors.json) already persists cursors crash-safely, so the claimed unbounded-growth/re-decrypt impact cannot occur in production; persistence is a documented follow-up to do if/when that E2EE path is ever wired.

title: The SQLCipher passphrase exists in exactly ONE un-escrowed local file — its loss = permanent, unrecoverable loss of the entire message DB; no backup/DR
mechanism: KeychainDBKey.loadOrCreate() generates a 256-bit key once and writes it to a single 0600 local file via LocalSecretStore (KeychainDBKey.swift:15-30, deliberately NOT the macOS Keychain), and OsmoDatabase.open AES-encrypts the whole DB with it (OsmoDatabase.swift:18-25). There is no escrow, no export, no recovery phrase, and Time Machine/any backup captures only ciphertext that becomes undecryptable if that key file is lost or corrupted (Migration Assistant skipping it, disk corruption, or the app's own token/keychain-clear paths). Because everything hinges on one un-backed-up secret, a routine OS event silently converts the user's entire relationship history into unrecoverable bytes — a DR hole the 'it's all safely on your Mac' framing hides.
breaksAt: Any single key-file loss; unrecoverable by construction (no key = no data, and no second copy exists).
reasoning: Mechanism holds. KeychainDBKey.loadOrCreate() (KeychainDBKey.swift:15-20) writes a single 256-bit key to Application Support/Osmo/.dbkey at 0600 via LocalSecretStore.write (LocalSecretStore.swift:22,31-35), explicitly NOT the macOS Keychain, and OsmoDatabase.open applies it as the whole-DB SQLCipher passphrase (OsmoDatabase.swift:26-28). No escrow, recovery phrase, or key export exists anywhere. If the key file is lost, loadOrCreate mints a fresh key so the pre-existing ciphertext DB cannot be reopened; the server keeps no durable copy (in-memory oplog wiped on restart; Supabase holds only users/devices/subscriptions/magic_links/web_sessions). Real and unmitigated. Two framing corrections: (1) The Time Machine claim is inaccurate — .dbkey sits in Application Support which Time Machine/Migration Assistant back up together with the DB, so a full restore recovers both; the real risk is isolated corruption/deletion of the key file or a partial/selective restore. (2) The claim UNDERSTATES impact: AppModel.openEncrypted (AppModel.swift:1749-1755) does not merely leave undecryptable bytes — on key/DB mismatch it removeItem()s the .db/-wal/-shm files and recreates an empty store, so a lost key file silently DESTROYS the ciphertext on next launch. A manual JSON exportData() exists (SettingsView.swift:220 to OsmoStore+Shell.swift:210 exportJSON) but is user-initiated with NO import/restore counterpart, so not automatic DR and never backs up the key. iMessage bodies are re-importable from Apple chat.db, but user-authored value (relationship_memory notes/facts/summaries, projects/milestones, person merges, enrichment) lives only in the Osmo DB and is truly lost. Nothing in plan phases 0-6 addresses key escrow or DB backup/recovery, so planStatus=unaddressed. Severity stays medium: total silent irreversible loss of core data, but gated behind a specific non-everyday trigger rather than the routine event the original framing implied.
refined: The SQLCipher passphrase lives in exactly one un-escrowed local file (Application Support/Osmo/.dbkey) with no key backup, export, or recovery phrase, so isolated loss/corruption of that file permanently orphans the encrypted DB, and AppModel.openEncrypted (AppModel.swift:1749-1755) worsens it by silently deleting the ciphertext when a freshly-minted key fails to open it, destroying user-authored relationship memory and projects that exist nowhere else.

title: The golden-set eval harness and the log-scrubbing privacy test — the ONLY proofs behind the quality-ceiling and 'keep none' claims — do not exist in the repo
mechanism: Phase 3 'golden-set eval' and Phase 0's 'log-scrubbing test' are cited as the falsifiable backing for the floor-gate/ceiling quality guarantees and the privacy claim. A repo-wide search finds no golden-set, eval harness, or scrub/redaction test (only vendor/GRDB false positives). So the quality bar ('S4 generate-2 → Haiku judge → S5 repair produces better drafts', 'evidence-gated ceiling') and the privacy assertion are today entirely unfalsifiable — there is no CI gate that would catch a prompt/config change (now shippable WITHOUT OTA via the registry) that silently regresses draft quality or starts leaking PII into logs.
breaksAt: Immediately — the guarantees are asserted now while the harness proving them is unbuilt; a bad config push regresses quality/privacy with nothing to catch it.
evidence: No matches for golden/eval/scrub/redact harness under Tests/ or web/tests/ (grep returns only vendor GRDB); web/app/api/config/flags/route.ts (config path that would need the gate)
reasoning: Factual core is TRUE: no golden-set/eval/scrub/redact harness exists in Tests/, web/tests/, web/lib, scripts/, or docs/ (grep returns zero real matches; only a SwiftUI .redacted placeholder and build/ audit artifacts), and CI (.github/workflows/release.yml) has no such gate. But the framing overstates it into a present hole for three reasons. (1) 'keep none' is enforced by CODE today, not merely by a missing test: web/app/api/suggest/route.ts:9,107 store nothing, and a grep for thread/body/userTurn/systemCore content logging across web/app+web/lib returns zero — there is no PII in logs for a scrub test to catch yet. (2) The 'un-OTA'd signed registry' that supposedly makes a silent regression shippable does NOT exist — web/app/api/config/flags/route.ts is only boolean env-var kill-switches (aiDrafting/autodraft/…), not a prompt/model registry; systemCore is still sent by the client (route.ts:44), so prompt changes today still require an OTA build. The trigger mechanism is unbuilt (plan Phase 2). (3) The quality ceiling (S4→Haiku judge→S5) is unbuilt (Phase 4), so there is no live guarantee to regress; MessageJudgeTests/GeneratorTests give partial unit coverage but not a regression golden set. Hence 'breaksAt: Immediately / entirely unfalsifiable now' is wrong — nothing regresses today. The genuine, useful point is a plan-sequencing dependency: the registry (Phase 2, configs without OTA) is scheduled BEFORE the eval gate (Phase 3), so if built in that order an un-gated regression window opens. That is a real but future/low-severity process risk, not a present exploitable weakness, so severity is revised medium→low. planStatus stays partial: the plan names both harnesses (Phase 3 eval, Phase 0 scrub) but Phase 0's elaboration only specifies the systemCore/Safety/auth fix and the eval is deferred until after the registry ships.
refined: The plan's own eval (Phase 3) and log-scrub (Phase 0) gates aren't built, and it sequences the un-OTA'd config registry (Phase 2) ahead of the eval gate — a real but future low-severity risk of an un-gated regression window; it is not a present hole, since the registry doesn't exist yet (prompts still ride OTA), the quality ceiling is unbuilt, and 'keep none' is already code-enforced (suggest/route.ts:107 persists/logs no thread content).

title: Three hand-maintained version-of-truth sources; /api/version already reports an OLDER build than what is shipped
mechanism: App update truth is the Sparkle appcast (Info.plist SUFeedURL → leftonread.in/appcast.xml, currently 0.2.1/build 3, appcast.xml). But there are two other independently hand-edited version sources: the build config (project.yml:44-45, 0.2.1/3) and the backend stub /api/version (version/route.ts:3-8) which STILL returns 0.2.0/build 2 — already stale below the shipped build. Nothing couples app build ↔ appcast ↔ backend version, so any consumer that trusts /api/version (a website 'latest', a future force-update/min-version gate) will misreport or mis-gate, and the drift is invisible because no test asserts the three agree.
breaksAt: Already diverged today; bites when a force-update or 'latest version' feature is wired to the stale endpoint.
reasoning: Verified the core facts: web/app/api/version/route.ts:3-8 returns 0.2.0/build 2; project.yml:44-45 sets MARKETING_VERSION 0.2.1 / CURRENT_PROJECT_VERSION 3; .build/appcast/appcast.xml reports sparkle:version 3 / 0.2.1; Info.plist:37-39 points SUFeedURL at leftonread.in/appcast.xml. So the /api/version stub is genuinely stale below the shipped build. However the framing is off on two points. (1) The 'three independent hand-maintained truths' count is overstated: Info.plist:19-22 derives version via $(MARKETING_VERSION)/$(CURRENT_PROJECT_VERSION) from project.yml, and appcast.xml is generated by scripts/publish-appcast.sh via Sparkle generate_appcast against the built .app — so project.yml -> Info.plist -> appcast is one coupled build chain; the only truly decoupled, hand-edited source is /api/version. It is effectively two truths, not three. (2) The endpoint is a currently-unconsumed dead stub — its own comment says 'the app's manual update-check stub (Sparkle later)', and a repo-wide grep across App/Sources/web finds no reader; the app updates via Sparkle/SUFeedURL, not /api/version. So the divergence causes zero harm today and is purely a latent footgun, which supports the claimed low severity. No coupling or test exists (unmitigated). Plan is unaddressed: Phase 0 covers only systemCore/Safety/auth, and Phase 2's signed registry is explicitly volatile config only, not app-version truth.
refined: The /api/version endpoint (web/app/api/version/route.ts:3-8) is a hand-edited, currently-unconsumed stub decoupled from the app's real version source of truth (project.yml:44-45 -> Info.plist -> generated appcast.xml) and is already stale (0.2.0/build 2 vs shipped 0.2.1/build 3) with no coupling or test — harmless today but a latent mis-gate footgun if a future force-update/min-version check ever reads it.

title: Backend outage silently swapped for client 'mock' text; autodraft persists literal "[mock] ..." as a ready-to-send draft
mechanism: GeneratorRouter.generate catches `URLError` and returns MockGenerator output, which is the literal string "[mock] on <their message>: here's the direct reply..." (Generator.swift:24-33). Any backend-unreachable condition (Render down, DNS, TLS, client timeout at the URLSession 60s default) surfaces as a URLError, so the router treats a real production outage identically to 'dev proxy not running' and returns mock prose. The interactive SuggestionStrip at least post-hoc detects `text.contains("[mock]")` and shows a DEMO badge (SuggestionStrip.swift:139), but the BACKGROUND autodraft path (considerAutodraft, AppModel.swift:868-876) does NOT: it takes `result?.set.takes.first?.text`, sees non-empty mock prose, and calls `store.saveDraft(text, forThread:, isAuto: true)` with no mock filter. The queue then presents "[mock] on ...: the lighter reply 🙂" as the auto-drafted reply. isMockMode (BackendClient.swift:76-78) is decided from register().mode == "mock" (keyless-ness), NOT reachability — a healthy production backend that is merely DOWN yields register throwing -> `(try?...)?.mode` == nil -> false, so autodraft's `!isMockMode` guard passes during the outage and the mock draft is written.
breaksAt: First inbound message during any Render outage or user network blip while the session's cached isMockMode is false; one-tap send from the queue then delivers 'mock' garbage to a real contact.
reasoning: Verified end-to-end. MockGenerator emits literal "[mock] on subject: ..." (Generator.swift:30-32). GeneratorRouter.generate catches URLError and silently returns mock (ClaudeProxyGenerator.swift:80-85); this path is live in production because RuntimeConfig defaults authToken to local-dev (non-empty) so liveGenerator is non-nil (ClaudeProxyGenerator.swift:129-132). SuggestionService.suggest routes through it (SuggestionService.swift:28). considerAutodraft persists takes.first.text via saveDraft(isAuto:true) with NO mock filter (AppModel.swift:870-874). The !isMockMode guard (AppModel.swift:849) does NOT block this during an outage: isMockMode is set ONCE at launch (AppModel.swift:593) from register().mode==mock (BackendClient.swift:76-78), i.e. keyless-ness not reachability, so a keyed production backend that is DOWN keeps it false. Autodraft UI surfaces (InboxView.swift:363-364, TodayView.swift:226-228) show isAuto drafts with no mock badge, unlike interactive SuggestionStrip.swift:139 which does detect it, confirming the mitigation gap. Devs even acknowledge mock==unreachable in the Settings test (SettingsView.swift:143) but only handle it there. Downgrading critical to medium: trigger is broad (per-inbound during any outage on eligible Pro threads) but harm is bounded, nothing auto-sends, the mock prefix is plainly visible in the draft before send, the queue card only navigates into the thread (TodayView.swift:271-275), and it self-heals when the backend returns. planStatus partial: the new pipeline S3 floor gate and Phase 4 cold-start/outage handling are adjacent hooks but no phase explicitly targets client-side mock-masking-of-outage, and Phase 0 elaboration is scoped to systemCore/Safety/auth only.
refined: During a transient backend/network outage GeneratorRouter silently falls back to MockGenerator (ClaudeProxyGenerator.swift:83) and considerAutodraft persists the literal "[mock] ..." string as an isAuto draft with no mock filter (AppModel.swift:870-874) because the isMockMode guard reads a launch-time reachability-blind flag (AppModel.swift:593), so eligible Pro threads accumulate visibly-broken ready auto-drafts during outages (bounded, since the mock prefix is user-visible and nothing auto-sends).

title: OAuth access tokens never refreshed: Gmail send dies ~1h after connect, X ~2h — refresh tokens are obtained then discarded
mechanism: sendGmail/sendX use the access_token captured at connect time and stored in-memory (send.ts:41,10; x/callback/route.ts:33 stores the whole bundle but the code only ever reads .access_token). Gmail access tokens expire ~1h, X ~2h. `refreshXToken` exists (providers.ts:124) but grep confirms it is never called anywhere, and there is NO Gmail/Slack refresh call at all — even though the auth URLs request refresh grants (Gmail access_type=offline+prompt=consent providers.ts:54-56; X offline.access providers.ts:43). After the token TTL, the provider returns 401, sendGmail/sendX throw, and /api/sync/send maps it to 422 'send rejected' (sync/send/route.ts:44-48). The connection is not even marked degraded on a send failure, so the UI shows 'connected' while every reply silently 422s until the user manually disconnects/reconnects.
breaksAt: Universally, ~1h/~2h after each connect; the app's core promise (draft + send a reply) is broken for Gmail/X within an hour of onboarding.
evidence: web/lib/oauth/providers.ts:124 (refreshXToken never called); web/lib/oauth/send.ts:10,41; web/app/api/oauth/x/callback/route.ts:33; web/app/api/sync/send/route.ts:44-48
reasoning: Mechanism verified line-by-line. sendGmail/sendX/sendSlack (send.ts:41,11,26) read only .access_token from the stored bundle. Both live callbacks persist the WHOLE bundle including refresh_token (x/callback:33, google/callback:31), yet refreshXToken (providers.ts:124) has zero call sites per grep, and no refreshGoogle/refreshSlack function exists at all. No expires_in/expiresAt tracking and no refresh scheduler anywhere. Gmail/X backfill is one-shot fire-and-forget at connect (callback:48/49) using the connect-time token, so nothing periodically re-touches or refreshes it. On expiry the provider returns a body lacking id/dm_event_id/ok, so the send functions throw and sync/send/route.ts:44-48 returns 422 'send rejected'; the catch never calls setConnectionStatus, and the only 'degraded' transitions live in the backfill paths (gmailBackfill.ts:238, xBackfill.ts:107, slackBackfill.ts:135), not on send failure — so the connection stays 'connected'. Net: Gmail send breaks ~1h and X DM send ~2h after connect, with the refresh token sitting unused. Minor framing caveats that don't change the verdict: the 422 is a visible client error (not a silent success), Slack xoxp user tokens generally don't expire so the Slack part of 'no refresh' isn't a real breakage, and this only bites live-OAuth deployments (keyless/mock default routes to the mock sender). High is justified because live OAuth is the production path and drafting+sending is the app's core promise, the failure is unrecoverable without manual disconnect/reconnect, and no connection-status signal surfaces it. Plan: no phase covers OAuth token refresh (Phase 0 elaboration is scoped to systemCore/Safety/auth), so unaddressed.
refined: In live-OAuth mode the proxy captures but never reads the OAuth refresh_token (refreshXToken exists but is uncalled; no Gmail/Slack refresh at all), so Gmail replies fail ~1h and X DMs ~2h after connect with a 422 while the connection stays marked 'connected' — the send path never refreshes the token nor marks the connection degraded.

title: No send idempotency key -> provider-succeeds-but-response-lost causes duplicate real messages to the recipient
mechanism: SendRequest carries only {platform, platformThreadID, text} — no idempotency key (types.ts:149). /api/sync/send calls the provider then mints a WireMessage with the provider-returned messageId (sync/send/route.ts:50-59). If the provider actually delivers the message but the HTTP response back to the server is lost (network reset, client-side URLSession 60s timeout on a slow Gmail send, Node undici bodyTimeout), the server's fetch throws and returns 422; the app shows 'send failed' and the user re-taps send. The oplog dedups on platformMessageID (comment sync/send/route.ts:1-4), but the SECOND provider send produces a NEW platformMessageID, so dedup never fires — the counterpart receives two copies of an intimate message. sendGmail also never checks res.ok on its thread-metadata lookup (send.ts:47-53), so a mid-flight token expiry there builds a malformed empty-recipient reply, compounding partial-send confusion.
breaksAt: Any flaky-network send; probability rises with large Gmail threads (metadata fetch + send round-trips push past client timeout).
reasoning: Verified the full path. SendRequest carries only {platform, platformThreadID, text} (types.ts:149) and the Swift client sends exactly those (BackendClient.swift:119-125); a grep for any idempotency/nonce/client_msg_id key across Sources/App/web returns nothing — no idempotency key exists end-to-end. /api/sync/send calls the provider then mints a WireMessage from the provider-returned messageId (route.ts:33-59). The only dedup is the oplog index keyed on `message:${platform}:${platformMessageID}` (memoryStore.ts:152,214), which is structurally incapable of catching a retry because each provider send produces a NEW platformMessageID — the claim's central point holds. The mechanism is actually stronger than claimed: AppModel.send (AppModel.swift:988-994) routes any non-4xx error (network reset / URLSession timeout while the server actually delivered) into a catch branch that QUEUES the send, and drainSendQueue (AppModel.swift:705-726) auto-resends on foreground/sync/next-send up to 5 attempts (line 722). So a lost success response on the app->server hop produces a duplicate provider delivery with no user action, and up to 5 copies if the app keeps missing the success ack. The 422 path (server->provider timeout -> user re-tap) is the milder branch. Impact for a relationship app whose whole value is careful high-stakes messaging: duplicate intimate messages reach real recipients; correctness/trust harm, not security/data-loss, and the trigger window (response lost after actual delivery) is a narrow-but-recurring subset of flaky sends — high, not critical. The secondary sendGmail res.ok gap (send.ts:47-50) is real but overstated: an empty To makes Gmail messages/send reject (no data.id -> throws at line 80), so no malformed message is delivered; it only yields a confusing error. Plan phases 0-6 and the MUST-FIX list address only systemCore/Safety/auth, never send idempotency — unaddressed.
refined: The send pipeline has no idempotency key anywhere, so when a provider send actually delivers but the success response is lost, the retry (automatic queue-drain on app->server loss, up to 5 attempts; or user re-tap on the server->provider 422 path) issues a fresh provider send whose new platformMessageID the oplog dedup cannot catch, delivering 1-5 duplicate real messages to the recipient.

title: Free-tier quota is consumed BEFORE the Anthropic call and never rolled back on failure; an Anthropic incident drains a user's weekly quota producing zero drafts
mechanism: In /api/suggest, checkAndConsume() bumps the weekly usage counter (suggest/route.ts:69) BEFORE the Anthropic fetch (line 88). If Anthropic returns non-2xx the route returns 502 (lines 98-100) with the quota already decremented and no rollback; there is no retry/backoff on the wire. During an Anthropic 429/529 overloaded incident (common and transient), each interactive draft / regenerate tap burns one of the free user's 15/week while returning an error. A short Anthropic blip can therefore exhaust a paying-adjacent free user's entire weekly allowance without delivering a single usable draft.
breaksAt: Free tier during any Anthropic degradation; a handful of retries on a flaky moment silently eats the 15/week cap.
evidence: web/app/api/suggest/route.ts:63-76 (consume) then :88-100 (fetch, 502 on failure, no rollback/retry); web/lib/license/quota.ts:28-38
reasoning: Mechanism confirmed against code. web/app/api/suggest/route.ts:69 calls checkAndConsume(); for free tier (unlimited=false) quota.ts:34-37 calls store.bumpUsage which increments the weekly counter (memoryStore.ts:283-289) BEFORE the Anthropic fetch at route.ts:88. On !res.ok the route returns 502 (route.ts:98-100) with no rollback; there is no decrement/refund API anywhere (grep shows only bumpUsage, increment-only) and no retry/backoff. The fetch at line 88 also lacks any try/catch, so a timeout/ECONNRESET throws an unhandled 500 with quota still consumed — reinforcing the timeout trigger. So an Anthropic 429/500/529/timeout burns a free user's draft (1 per failed POST/regenerate) while delivering nothing. Not mitigated by existing code. HOWEVER the claimed 'high' + 'a short blip exhausts the entire 15/week' overstates impact: each failure consumes exactly one, so full exhaustion requires ~15 taps; the counter is in-memory and wiped on restart (transient, self-healing on deploy); and consuming quota on a failed call costs the company nothing (no billing/data-integrity harm — if anything it over-protects the bill). This is a real consume-before-commit-without-rollback UX bug affecting the free acquisition funnel during upstream degradation, best rated medium. Plan status: Phase 0's elaboration covers only the systemCore/Safety/auth MUST-FIX; Phase 4's outage handling targets the S4 background quality gate, not the interactive free-tier quota; no phase specifies metering at successful S6 deliver or refunding on failure — unaddressed.
refined: In /api/suggest the free-tier weekly quota is bumped (memoryStore.bumpUsage) before the un-retried, un-try/caught Anthropic fetch and is never refunded on a 502 non-2xx or a thrown timeout, so each upstream Anthropic error burns one of a free user's 15 weekly drafts with no draft delivered — a real consume-before-commit bug, though impact is bounded (one per failed tap, transient in-memory counter reset on restart, no billing/data harm) rather than a single blip wiping the whole allowance.

title: Process restart mid-backfill = total connection + half-imported-history loss; the promised 'reconciliation poller' does not exist for OAuth providers
mechanism: backfillGmail/Slack/X and backfillConnection are fired as unawaited `void` promises inside the OAuth-callback request process (google/slack/x callbacks; accounts/route.ts:48). backfill.ts:22 claims 'failures leave the connection backfilling and the reconciliation poller retries', but the only restart-heal is adoptOrphanedAccounts, which is (a) gated behind OSMO_ADOPT_ORPHANS=1, off by default, and (b) Unipile-live-only — it never re-runs Gmail/Slack/X OAuth backfills (accounts/route.ts:24-30). Since connections, OAuth tokens, and the per-device oplog are all in-memory, a Render redeploy/restart mid-import wipes them; the app then gets 401 -> re-registers -> cursor reset -> re-pulls from seq 0 into an EMPTY oplog. The half-imported history is gone and the connection object no longer exists, so the user must re-OAuth every provider — on every deploy. The `degraded` state the catch blocks set is itself in-memory and evaporates on the same restart.
breaksAt: Every Render redeploy that overlaps an active backfill; on a frequently-restarting single instance, users are forced to reconnect providers repeatedly.
reasoning: Mechanism largely holds. All OAuth callbacks fire unawaited backfills (google/callback:48, slack/callback:53, x/callback:49; also accounts/route:48, rebackfill:26). memoryStore.ts is a globalThis Map singleton — connections, OAuth tokens, oplog, device-token map are all in-memory and wiped on restart; the durable Supabase accounts store (accounts/store.ts) persists ONLY devices/users/subscriptions, never connections/tokens/oplog. connections/auth.ts:19 validates the token against the in-memory device map ONLY, so a restart forces 401 -> re-register -> onReRegistered resets cursor to "" (RealtimeSyncEngine.swift:132-136). The 'reconciliation poller' in backfill.ts:22,153 does NOT exist as a server-side backfill retry — it's the app's 60s pullNow which only re-pulls the oplog by cursor and never re-pages Gmail/Slack/X. adoptOrphanedAccounts is off by default (route.ts:25), Unipile-live-only (route.ts:30), calls the Unipile backfillConnection (never the OAuth backfills), and has no tokens post-restart; rebackfill needs an existing connection (409 after restart). So on EVERY restart all connections+tokens vanish and the user must re-OAuth every provider — broader than the 'mid-backfill' title implies. The claim is materially WRONG on one point: 'half-imported history is gone.' Already-imported rows live in the Mac's local-first encrypted store; cursor reset + the additive/idempotent pullNow ingest (RealtimeSyncEngine.swift:222-245) never deletes local data, and the remainder is re-fetched idempotently after re-OAuth — so there is NO permanent message loss. planStatus partial: Phase 0 is titled 'Security & durable state' (which would fix this) but its elaboration only specifies the systemCore/Safety/auth MUST-FIX, leaving connection/token/oplog durability and backfill resumption unspecified.
refined: Because connections + OAuth tokens live only in the in-memory store and the message-sync auth path (auth.ts:19) checks only that in-memory device map, every server restart wipes all connections and forces a 401 -> re-register -> cursor reset, so users must re-OAuth every provider and any in-flight import is aborted with no server-side resume (the 'reconciliation poller' only re-pulls the oplog, never re-pages OAuth history) — but already-imported messages survive on the Mac's local-first store and the remainder is re-fetched idempotently on reconnect, so there is no permanent history loss.

title: Gmail/Slack/X have no inbound path after the one-shot backfill — every new message is a permanently 'missed webhook'
mechanism: The OAuth backfills are one-shot imports (gmailBackfill: a single messages.list over `newer_than:Nd`, no history.list/users.watch Pub/Sub; slackBackfill: conversations.history once; xBackfill: once). There is NO Gmail push watch, NO Slack Events subscription, NO X polling, and no cron/worker (render.yaml has no cron; instrumentation.ts only ensures Unipile hooks). The only live inbound path is /api/webhooks/unipile, which matches connectionById(account_id) and only ever fires for Unipile-managed platforms (LinkedIn/WhatsApp/Instagram) — it never delivers Gmail/Slack/X messages. Consequently, after the initial import the app's own email/DM channels silently stop updating: new inbound Gmail/Slack/X messages never append to the oplog, so autodraft-on-arrival and threadIntel never fire for them and the inbox looks frozen for those providers until a manual rebackfill.
breaksAt: Immediately post-backfill for every OAuth provider; 100% of subsequent messages on those channels are missed.
evidence: web/lib/oauth/gmailBackfill.ts:147-249 (one-shot, no watch); web/app/api/webhooks/unipile/route.ts:19-31 (connectionById match, Unipile-only); render.yaml (no cron); web/instrumentation.ts
reasoning: Mechanism holds as read. All three OAuth imports are strictly one-shot: gmailBackfill.ts:147 (messages.list over newer_than:Nd, no users.watch/Pub-Sub/history.list), slackBackfill.ts:56 (conversations.list+history, no Events API/socket), xBackfill.ts:25 (dm_events paging, no polling). Grep confirms each is invoked ONLY from its oauth/*/callback route — i.e. exactly once at connect. render.yaml has a single always-on web service with NO cron/worker/schedule (verified). instrumentation.ts registers only Unipile webhooks (webhooks.ts). /api/webhooks/unipile/route.ts:20 matches store.connectionById(account_id) and Unipile manages only LinkedIn/WhatsApp/Instagram, so it never delivers gmail-*/slack/x connections. The 'reconciliation timer' referenced in events.ts:4 and types.ts:136 is the CLIENT pulling the SERVER oplog via /api/sync/pull (realtime.pullNow at AppModel.swift:886) — it never makes the server re-fetch providers, so a provider that never appends new rows stays frozen. I checked two candidate mitigations and both fail: (a) the on-device SyncCoordinator.swift DOES contain live Gmail/Slack fetch loops, but it is constructed bare at AppModel.swift:550 (SyncCoordinator(store: store)), nothing anywhere assigns syncCoordinator.credentials or constructs APICredentials with real tokens, there is no on-device OAuth wiring (grep for loopback/PKCE/oauth in App/Sources returns nothing), and the server explicitly stores gmail/slack/x tokens server-side and never sends them to the app (google/callback comment) — so gmailReady/slackReady are permanently false and the path is dead code (and X isn't handled at all); (b) rebackfill (connect/rebackfill/route.ts) routes ALL platforms through the Unipile backfillConnection, not the OAuth backfills, so it can't even re-import Gmail/Slack/X — it would call unipile.listMessages with a gmail-* id and mark the connection degraded. Net: after the connect-time snapshot, 100% of subsequent inbound Gmail/Slack/X messages never reach the oplog, so autodraft-on-arrival and threadIntel never fire for them. The plan's phases 0-6 concern the drafting pipeline (FACTS/JUDGMENT/PERSONALIZATION) plus security/durable-state; none establish a continuous ingestion (watch/Events/poll/cron) path, so this is unaddressed. Severity high, not critical: it is a total functional gap for 3 of ~6 channels but is recoverable (disconnect+reconnect re-runs backfill), causes no data corruption or security exposure, and Unipile-managed platforms remain live via webhook+reconciliation. Only correction to the claim: the stated recovery 'until a manual rebackfill' is inaccurate — rebackfill does NOT re-import OAuth providers; only a full disconnect+reconnect does — which makes the gap slightly worse, not better.
refined: Gmail/Slack/X connect via one-shot OAuth backfills invoked only at their callback routes (gmailBackfill.ts:147 / slackBackfill.ts:56 / xBackfill.ts:25) with no watch/Events/polling subscription, no cron in render.yaml, and instrumentation.ts wiring only Unipile webhooks whose /api/webhooks/unipile handler matches Unipile-only account ids — so every inbound message on those three channels after connect is permanently dropped from the oplog (the on-device SyncCoordinator's Gmail/Slack fetch path is dead because it never receives credentials, and rebackfill routes OAuth providers through the Unipile path so it can't re-import them; only disconnect+reconnect recovers).

title: Supabase sits on the critical path of every paid draft with unhandled failure: a Supabase blip silently downgrades Pro users to free (429) or 500s all drafting
mechanism: /api/suggest awaits getAccounts().subscriptionForDevice(device.id) with no try/catch, no timeout, no cache (suggest/route.ts:67) purely to decide the quota tier — drafting itself needs nothing from Supabase. The Supabase implementation destructures only `{ data }` and ignores the `error` field (store.ts:291-293), so a Supabase query error resolves to `mapSub(null) -> freeSub`. Result: while Supabase is degraded, a Pro/trial user is silently treated as FREE, hits checkAndConsume, and is 429'd 'quota_exceeded' after 15 drafts/week despite paying. If instead the underlying fetch hard-rejects (network fully down), the un-try/catch'd await throws and Next returns 500, taking ALL drafting offline. Either failure mode is a silent wrong-answer coupling drafting availability to an unrelated dependency.
breaksAt: Pro/trial users during any Supabase degradation get throttled to free; a hard reject 500s the whole draft path.
reasoning: Core mechanism holds. suggest/route.ts:67 awaits subscriptionForDevice with no try/catch/timeout/cache, solely to choose the quota tier.
