In SupabaseAccountsStore (store.ts:291-294) both queries (ownerFor->deviceById :235-238 and the subscription select :293) destructure only {data} and ignore {error}; mapSub(null) -> freeSub (:224-227) -> resolveTier="free" (entitlement.ts:24) -> checkAndConsume(unlimited=false) applies the 15/week cap (quota.ts:28-37) -> a Pro/trial user is silently treated as free and 429'd once over the cap. This is a genuine fail-closed billing/entitlement-correctness coupling to an unrelated dependency; NOT mitigated anywhere. However the claim's second framing ('hard reject -> Next 500s all drafting') is inaccurate for the client actually installed: @supabase/supabase-js@2.110.0 / postgrest-js catches all fetch errors (network-down, timeout, abort, 5xx) in PostgrestBuilder.then when shouldThrowOnError is false — the default, since the code never calls .throwOnError() (verified index.cjs:328-369) — resolving to {data:null,error,status:0} rather than rejecting. So an outage does NOT throw/500; both claimed modes collapse into one silent downgrade-to-free. Severity revised high->medium because harm requires the confluence of a Supabase outage AND a paying user exceeding 15 drafts since the last restart (usage counter is in the in-memory sync store, wiped on restart); under-cap Pro users keep drafting. Note the identical pattern in license/validate/route.ts:22-23 would sign a 7-day free entitlement during a blip, broadening the pattern. Plan Phase 0 elaboration only covers the systemCore/Safety/auth fix, so unaddressed.
refined: /api/suggest picks the paid tier via an unguarded Supabase read (no try/catch/timeout/cache) whose error field is ignored, so during any Supabase degradation a Pro/trial device is silently mapped to freeSub and 429'd once over the 15/week cap — a fail-closed billing-correctness coupling (the claimed 'HTTP 500s all drafting' mode does not occur, since supabase-js resolves fetch errors to {data:null} instead of rejecting).

title: No timeout/circuit-breaker on the server Anthropic fetch; one slow-Anthropic incident can exhaust the single always-on instance shared by all 7 LLM call sites
mechanism: The proxy's fetch to Anthropic has no AbortSignal/timeout (suggest/route.ts:88-96) and Node undici defaults leave headers/body timeouts high (~300s), so a hung Anthropic request holds a server request open far longer than the client's 60s URLSession default. The client times out at 60s -> URLError -> falls to mock, but the server socket lingers. All 7 LLM call sites (suggest/judge/threadIntel/insight/dossier/ask/voicePersona) funnel through this one blocking, non-streaming wire onto ONE render-pinned instance with no global spend cap or circuit breaker; threadIntel fires one call per new inbound and autodraft up to 30/day, so during an Anthropic slowdown a burst of long-lived hung requests can saturate the single instance's concurrency and stall unrelated API routes (sync/pull, accounts, SSE) that share the process.
breaksAt: Single-instance concurrency ceiling during any sustained Anthropic slowdown; the whole backend (not just drafting) degrades because state and LLM share one process.
evidence: web/app/api/suggest/route.ts:88-96 (no signal/timeout, no retry); Sources/OsmoBrain/ClaudeProxyGenerator.swift:33-37 (no request timeout); render.yaml (one instance)
reasoning: Cited facts all verified. web/app/api/suggest/route.ts:88-96 issues the Anthropic fetch with no AbortSignal/timeout/retry/circuit-breaker; the route sets runtime="nodejs" with no maxDuration and never wires req.signal into the fetch, so a client disconnect does not cancel the upstream call. grep confirms this is the ONLY api.anthropic.com fetch in the backend, and all 7 LLM call sites (SuggestionService, MessageJudge/judge, ThreadIntel, Insight, Dossier, Ask, VoiceStats/voicePersona — all calling generator.generate() via ClaudeProxyGenerator) funnel through it. ClaudeProxyGenerator.swift:33-37 uses URLSession.shared with no custom timeout, so the 60s default applies and GeneratorRouter (81-85) catches URLError -> mock; the client recovers but the server outbound call lingers (undici default ~300s). render.yaml:9-16 confirms one always-on starter (~512MB) instance whose comment states all state is in-memory + SSE. So the core weakness is real and NOT mitigated server-side (client 60s+mock, quota checkAndConsume, and AutodraftPolicy.dailyCap=30 bound UX and per-device burst but do nothing for the server-side lingering fetch or upstream fan-out). Marked revised, not confirmed, because the failure framing is imprecise for Node: an awaited fetch does not block the event loop or 'saturate concurrency'/'stall unrelated routes' the way a thread-per-request server would. The genuine whole-backend degradation path is memory pressure from many in-flight requests each holding the large systemCore prompt + buffers for up to ~300s (vs the abandoned 60s client) plus unbounded upstream fan-out with no spend cap, which can OOM-crash the single process and wipe ALL in-memory state (oplog, OAuth tokens, quota, SSE subscribers, pending links) for every user. Severity medium stands: high-impact consequence but gated by needing sustained cross-user volume on a small free-first user base. planStatus partial: Phase 0 names 'durable state' (which, if built, would remove the crash-wipe amplification) but its elaboration covers only systemCore/Safety/auth, and Phase 4 mentions cold-start/outage handling in the background quality-gate/client context; no phase specifies a server-side request timeout, circuit breaker, or global spend cap on the Anthropic fetch.
refined: The server's Anthropic fetch (suggest/route.ts:88-96) has no timeout, circuit breaker, or spend cap and isn't tied to req.signal, so during an Anthropic slowdown many requests linger ~300s (vs the client's abandoned 60s) holding large prompts+buffers with unbounded upstream fan-out on the single 512MB starter instance, risking a memory/OOM crash that wipes all in-memory state (oplog/OAuth/quota/SSE/links) for every user.

title: Auth validates the device token against ephemeral memory, so every redeploy orphans the durable device + its subscription (split-brain between memoryStore.devices and accounts.osmo_devices)
mechanism: requireDevice() resolves the bearer token ONLY via getStore().deviceByToken() — the in-memory MemoryConnectionsStore.devices map (token -> Device). The durable AccountsStore (Supabase osmo_devices) DOES persist the same {id, token} (upsertDevice writes token), but the AccountsStore interface exposes only deviceById — there is NO deviceByToken, so auth can never fall back to the durable copy. After any restart/redeploy the memory devices map is empty, so EVERY authenticated request 401s. The Swift client's authed() treats 401 as 'backend forgot me': it dropCredentials() (clears the Keychain token), then POSTs /api/device/register, which ALWAYS mints a brand-new dev-<uuid> + token (there is no re-register-as-existing-id path). The client now runs under a NEW deviceId whose durable subscription row does not exist (owner_type=device, owner_id=oldDeviceId is orphaned; owner_type=user requires a manual /api/account/link re-run). The old device row lingers forever; every redeploy leaks another orphan.
breaksAt: Every user on every deploy — Render redeploys on each push; the one pinned instance has no shared/durable token store.
reasoning: Mechanism verified end-to-end. auth.ts:19 resolves the bearer token ONLY through getStore().deviceByToken() (in-memory MemoryConnectionsStore.devices, token->Device), which getStore() rebuilds as freshState() on every cold start (memoryStore.ts:337) with no boot hydration. The durable AccountsStore persists {id,token} via upsertDevice (store.ts:229-234, register/route.ts:13) but exposes only deviceById — grep confirms NO deviceByToken exists in accounts, so auth has no durable fallback. On restart the memory map is empty, so every authed app request 401s (requireDevice is the shared auth; /api/suggest does the same inline check at route.ts:33 under OSMO_REQUIRE_AUTH=1). Swift authed() (BackendClient.swift:330-332) treats 401 as dropCredentials() (tokenStore.clear(), line 285) then register(), which always mints a fresh dev-<uuid>+token (memoryStore.ts:160-168) — no re-register-as-existing-id path. The new unlinked id resolves to freeSub (store.ts:287-295), orphaning the durable owner_type=device subscription and leaking the old osmo_devices row; account-linked users need a manual /api/account/link re-run. Not mitigated: no deviceByToken, no hydration, no id-preserving re-register. The suggest route comment (route.ts:60-62) claiming 'account-level Pro unlocks even on a device whose ephemeral state was reset' is actually defeated by this bug, since the reset forces a new unlinked id. Severity revised critical->high: real and recurs on every redeploy/crash, silently downgrading paid/linked users and leaking orphan rows, but the client self-heals connectivity, message data is local-first (no data loss), free/trial users are barely affected (even re-trialed), and the state is recoverable — serious billing/identity-integrity, not catastrophic. planStatus partial: Phase 0 names 'durable state' but its elaboration only covers the systemCore/Safety/keyless-auth fix, not durable token->device resolution.
refined: requireDevice() resolves bearer tokens only against the wiped-on-restart in-memory device map (auth.ts:19) with no durable deviceByToken fallback in AccountsStore, so every Render redeploy 401s all devices and the Swift client silently re-registers under a new dev-<uuid> (BackendClient.swift:331-332, register/route.ts:9-13), orphaning the durable subscription bound to the old device id and leaking the old osmo_devices row on every deploy.

title: Stripe cancellation is a lost write to a disconnected store, so cancelled Pro never lapses and the 7-day offline grace never counts down
mechanism: customer.subscription.deleted in /api/webhooks/stripe calls getStore().setLicense(deviceId,{subscriptionActive:false}) — writing to the EPHEMERAL memoryStore.licenses map. But /api/license/validate resolves tier from getAccounts().subscriptionForDevice() — the DURABLE Supabase osmo_subscriptions table. These are two different stores; nothing ever writes subscriptionActive=false into the durable store on a billing event. So the durable record stays active forever. Because buildSignedEntitlement re-issues expiresAt = now + OFFLINE_GRACE_DAYS(7) on every validate, and the client re-validates online periodically, the offline-grace window is perpetually refreshed off a stale 'active' — the grace clock never starts ticking down after cancellation. Compounded: the webhook also no-ops unless store.deviceById(deviceId) is truthy, which after any restart it is not (memory devices empty), so even the wrong-store write is frequently dropped.
breaksAt: Every paying user who cancels — revenue leak is permanent, not time-bounded; and manifests the moment real Stripe is wired.
reasoning: Mechanism verified end-to-end. /api/webhooks/stripe:22,29,37 writes subscription state via getStore().setLicense — the ephemeral ConnectionsStore.licenses map (memoryStore.ts:269-273, wiped on restart). /api/license/validate:22 resolves tier from getAccounts().subscriptionForDevice — the durable Supabase osmo_subscriptions table (accounts/store.ts:291-295). These are genuinely two disjoint stores. I grepped every setSubscriptionForDevice/setSubscriptionForUser caller: the ONLY durable writes of subscriptionActive:false are /api/license/reset (dev-only, 404s once STRIPE_SECRET_KEY is set) and account/delete->purgeByDevice (full deletion). No billing/lapse event ever deactivates the durable record, so it stays active forever. resolveTier returns pro while subscriptionActive (entitlement.ts:19) and buildSignedEntitlement reissues expiresAt=issuedAt+7d on every validate (entitlement.ts:34); the OFFLINE_GRACE_DAYS comment even states the 7 days is meant to let a cancelled sub lapse within a week — which is defeated because cancellation never reaches the durable store, so each online re-validation refreshes a fresh grace off a stale active. The compounding store.deviceById no-op guard is also confirmed (memoryStore.ts:170), though secondary. Not mitigated anywhere. Severity revised critical->high because the entire Stripe path is explicit scaffold: checkout/session falls through to mock even with STRIPE_SECRET_KEY, and the webhook 200s as a no-op without STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET (constructEvent is a TODO). The bug is dormant today and manifests only when real Stripe is wired — in the very file an implementer would be editing — so it is a real, permanent revenue-integrity defect to fix before billing goes live, not an active production breach. planStatus partial: Phase 0 is themed 'Security & durable state' and slated first, but its elaboration only specifies the systemCore/Safety/auth fix and never names this durable-store cancellation lost-write.
refined: The durable store has no subscription-cancellation/lapse path at all: the sole scaffolded handler (customer.subscription.deleted) writes subscriptionActive=false into the ephemeral memoryStore via getStore().setLicense while /api/license/validate reads tier from the durable getAccounts() Supabase subscription, so once real Stripe is wired every cancellation is a lost write, resolveTier keeps returning pro, and buildSignedEntitlement keeps reissuing a fresh 7-day offline grace so the user never lapses.

title: Rejected identity merges resurrect: merge_decision is device-local (never synced) AND its pairKey is unstable under cluster membership change
mechanism: Two independent defects. (1) Cross-device: rejectMergePair writes to the merge_decision table, which is absent from OsmoStore.syncTables (only contact/person/thread/project/relationship_memory/message sync) and carries no SyncMeta columns — so a rejection recorded on device A never propagates. Device B, which DOES receive the synced person/contact rows, re-runs rebuildIdentityGraph and re-surfaces the exact pair the user already dismissed on A, forever. (2) Single-device: pairKey(a,b) is built from each cluster's MIN contact UUID, and resolve() reconstructs the lookup key from the CURRENT cluster's min (reps[i]). When a cluster gains a member sharing a global key (e.g. connecting WhatsApp adds a same-phone contact whose UUID is smaller) or loses its current-min member (tombstoned via the isDeleted filter, or flagged isMe), the min changes, the reconstructed key no longer equals the stored key, and the rejected suggestion reappears. The code comment claiming 'same phone/email => same contacts => same min => survives rebuilds' is false whenever cluster membership changes.
breaksAt: Multi-device users always (cross-device); single-device users the first time a rejected cluster's min contact changes — common as more platforms are connected.
reasoning: Both mechanisms hold against the code. (1) Cross-device: `merge_decision` is excluded from `OsmoStore.syncTables` (OsmoStore+Sync.swift:7-8) and has no SyncMeta columns (OsmoDatabase.swift:185-189), so `exportChanges` (which only walks syncTables) can never propagate it; `rejectedMergePairKeys()` reads the local-only table (OsmoStore.swift:479-485) and `rebuildIdentityGraph()` feeds it into resolve (OsmoStore.swift:402), and rebuild runs on the peer after synced contact/person rows land (RealtimeSyncEngine.swift:217/256/262; AppModel.swift:917). Confirmed merges DO propagate via synced personID, but rejections do not — asymmetry re-surfaces the dismissed pair on device B. (2) Single-device: pairKey uses the cluster's min UUID (IdentityResolver.swift:55-59) and resolve reconstructs the lookup from the CURRENT cluster min reps[i] (lines 116,164-167); the live filter drops isDeleted/isMe (line 69) and union-find can add a same-global-key contact with a smaller UUID, either of which changes the min so the stored key no longer matches and the rejected suggestion reappears — the stability comment (lines 51-54) is only valid when membership is constant. Neither path is mitigated anywhere. Severity revised down from high to medium: the resolver never auto-merges (lines 27-28,172-177 emit review suggestions only), so the impact is a recurring, non-destructive, self-recoverable re-prompt — no data loss, no wrong silent merge, no security impact — though it is permanent for multi-device users. Plan Phase 0 durable-state work concerns the web proxy's in-memory server state, not the on-device sync-table set or key stability, so this is unaddressed.
refined: A user's "not the same person" rejection is non-durable — merge_decision is device-local (excluded from syncTables, no SyncMeta) so it never syncs and the pair reappears on any other device after rebuild, and even on one device the rejection key (cluster min UUID) breaks whenever a smaller-UUID same-key contact is added or the current-min member is deleted/flagged isMe — but the effect is a recurring non-destructive re-prompt, never an incorrect or silent merge.

title: Deterministic Person IDs are minted from a cluster's MIN contact UUID, so two devices converging out-of-order create two Person rows for one real person (identity split-brain under LWW)
mechanism: rebuildIdentityGraph mints a new Person id = DeterministicID.v5('person:' + cluster.min(uuidString)). Contact IDs are deterministic (v5 of platform+handle) so a real person's contacts share UUIDs across devices — BUT the MIN of the cluster depends on WHICH contacts a device has ingested at rebuild time. During initial sync convergence, device A may see {c1,c2} (min c1 -> personID=v5(person:c1)) while device B, mid-pull, sees only {c2} (min c2 -> personID=v5(person:c2)). Each links its contacts to a different personID, stamps deviceSeq/updatedAt, and syncs. applyChanges is last-writer-wins by updatedAt: both Person rows now exist on both devices, and each contact's personID is whichever device wrote it later — so the single real person is split across two Person records. RelationshipMemory and Project rows (keyed by personID) split with it; the enrichment (keyed by personID) orphans. rebuildIdentityGraph's 'reuse existingPID = members.first' only pins the FIRST assignment per device; it cannot reconcile two devices that each assigned first.
breaksAt: Any multi-device account during the initial backfill window, or whenever a contact arrives on one device before the other.
reasoning: Code confirms the raw mechanism: rebuildIdentityGraph (OsmoStore.swift:419) mints personID = v5("person:" + cluster.min(uuidString)); the min is over WHICH contacts are present at mint time, so it is non-deterministic under a partial cluster view. Contacts are deterministically ID'd (OsmoContact.swift:51-53) and multi-contact clusters really form for a shared global key — same phone(last10) or email across two platforms/handle-forms (IdentityResolver.swift:83-95, HandleNormalizer.swift:12-16); username handles stay singletons (min=self) and can never split. applyChanges is INSERT OR REPLACE + LWW by updatedAt (OsmoStore+Sync.swift:59-75), and two Person rows with different ids both survive. So during initial-sync convergence two devices CAN concurrently first-mint two different Person rows for one cluster — the transient two-Person state is real. HOWEVER the claim's core assertion that it 'cannot reconcile two devices that each assigned first' is false. clusters are sorted by uuidString (IdentityResolver.swift:94-95), so members order is deterministic across devices; on any subsequent rebuild after both contacts co-exist, existingPID = members.compactMap(\.personID).first picks one survivor and relinks the other member (OsmoStore.swift:410,426-430), and both devices pick the SAME survivor because contact rows have already LWW-converged. The contact->person linkage self-heals; it is NOT a permanent split. The genuine residual harm is narrower: (1) the losing Person row is never tombstoned (only mergePeople at :450-453 tombstones; rebuild does not), leaving an orphan empty Person that syncs; (2) any RelationshipMemory (id==personID), Project(personID) or PersonEnrichment(id==personID) written against the losing personID during the convergence window becomes orphaned after contacts relink to the winner. Also note SyncEngine.pull() (SyncEngine.swift:47) applies LWW with no rebuild, so reconciliation relies on the pullNow/local-ingest debounce (RealtimeSyncEngine:217) firing. The reuse-existing logic partially mitigates (steady-state stability + eventual contact self-heal) but does not tombstone the loser or prevent the transient split, so not fully mitigated. Plan Phase 0 elaboration covers only systemCore/Safety/auth; no phase addresses ID-mint stability, so unaddressed. Severity revised high->medium: real correctness/data-orphaning bug but self-healing for contacts and with a bounded blast radius (multi-contact phone/email clusters, concurrent first-mint in the initial-backfill window, on an account that usually has little person-keyed data yet).
refined: Minting personID from a cluster's MIN contact UUID is non-deterministic under partial cluster views, so two devices concurrently first-minting during initial-sync convergence create two Person rows for one person; the contact links self-heal on the next rebuild (reuse-existing + uuid-sorted clusters pick the same survivor on both devices), but the losing Person row is never tombstoned and any RelationshipMemory/Project/PersonEnrichment keyed to it during that window is orphaned.

title: A server redeploy instantly drops paying users to Free (defeating the 7-day offline grace) because re-registration mints a new deviceId that fails the entitlement's device binding
mechanism: The signed entitlement is bound to a specific deviceId (payload.deviceId), and EntitlementVerifier.verify returns nil whenever expectedDeviceID != payload.deviceId. When a redeploy forces re-registration (see device-identity finding), the client's expectedDeviceID becomes the NEW dev-<uuid>, so its cached, still-time-valid Pro/trial entitlement fails the device-mismatch check and verify() returns nil -> the app treats itself as Free IMMEDIATELY. The whole point of OFFLINE_GRACE_DAYS=7 (keep Pro through an offline trip / brief outage) is nullified: instead of gracefully honoring the cached signature, a mere backend restart revokes entitlement, and the new deviceId has no durable subscription to re-issue Pro. The user's paid state silently evaporates until (and only if) they manually re-run Sign in with Apple to re-link.
breaksAt: Every Pro/trial user on every deploy; worse than a true outage because the app has a valid signed grace token it refuses to honor under the new id.
reasoning: Outcome is real but the stated mechanism misreads the code. The cached/offline load path (AppModel.swift:319-326) calls verify() with expectedDeviceID:nil, so the device-mismatch guard (EntitlementVerifier.swift:60) is SKIPPED — the comment at 322-323 says binding is re-checked only on the async refresh. Thus a redeploy that changes the deviceId does NOT make the cached entitlement fail verify() nor drop the user "IMMEDIATELY/offline." During a genuine outage, refreshEntitlement (AppModel.swift:207-211) bails on the failed validateLicense, and the cached Pro entitlement is preserved — offline grace WORKS, contradicting the "7-day grace nullified" framing. The actual harm arises differently: requireDevice (web/lib/connections/auth.ts:19) validates tokens only against the in-memory store, so a restart forces 401→re-register→new dev-${uuid} with userId:null (memoryStore.ts:162; register/route.ts:10). On the next SUCCESSFUL refresh, subscriptionForDevice resolves owner to device:newId (accounts/store.ts:287-295), finds no row, returns freeSub, and validate/route.ts:23 signs a VALID FREE entitlement bound to the new id — so verify() PASSES (expectedDeviceID==payload.deviceId) and applyEntitlement (AppModel.swift:213-223) overwrites cached Pro with Free. This hits even signed-in users since launch/foreground (AppModel.swift:594,697) call only refreshEntitlement, never re-run linkAccount; the durable user:${userId} sub in Supabase is intact but unreachable until manual re-Sign-in. So real, but it is a consequence of the device-identity root cause (ephemeral in-memory device-token auth), not the entitlement device-binding check; severity reduced to medium as it is a downstream duplicate whose fix lives in the device-identity/durable-auth item. Plan Phase 0 names "durable state" but the elaboration covers only systemCore/Safety/auth, not device-identity/subscription survival, so partial.
refined: A backend redeploy forces re-registration (ephemeral in-memory device-token auth) into a new unlinked deviceId, so the next successful online entitlement refresh resolves that device to a free subscription and overwrites the cached Pro entitlement — dropping paying (even signed-in) users to Free until they manually re-Sign-in; this is a consequence of the device-identity root cause, not the entitlement device-binding check (which is bypassed offline via expectedDeviceID:nil and satisfied online), and offline grace during a true outage still holds.

title: Client backend cursor only resets on 401/re-register; migrating the per-process oplog to a durable/multi-instance seq space (Phase 0) will silently skip or double-deliver rows
mechanism: The oplog seq is a per-process counter (seqs map, seq += 1 in appendRows) reset to 0 on restart; pull() returns rows with seq > since. The client persists the returned cursor opaquely and resets it to '' ONLY via onReRegistered (i.e. on a 401). The plan's Phase-0 'durable state' aims to keep device tokens valid across restarts — precisely the case where NO 401 fires. If the durable oplog re-numbers seqs (a fresh DB sequence, or a per-device sequence with different values than the pre-migration in-memory counter), an existing client holding cursor=N will call pull(since=N): durable rows minted with seq <= N are never delivered (permanent silent gap), or if the new space starts higher, older content is re-shipped. Because the only cursor-reset trigger is re-registration, and durable auth deliberately avoids re-registration, cursors are never invalidated at the cutover. Multi-instance amplifies it: each instance's per-process counter diverges, so the same device pulling from instance A then B sees non-monotonic seqs and misses rows.
breaksAt: At the Phase-0/Phase-2 migration and at any horizontal scale-out (both explicit goals of the redesign).
reasoning: Verified against code. Client cursor reset fires ONLY via onReRegistered (RealtimeSyncEngine.swift:134-136), which is triggered only by register() on first launch or a 401 retry (BackendClient.swift:71,332) — no periodic resync, no other reset. Server seq is a per-process counter reset to 0 on restart (memoryStore.ts:209,217,227 + freshState at :336-339), and pull() (memoryStore.ts:231-247) returns rows with seq>since with NO stale-cursor guard; maxSeq() is defined (:248) but never consumed by any route (grep-confirmed). Crucially, auth.ts:19 authenticates every app route via the in-memory deviceByToken, NOT the durable osmo_devices table (which already exists in accounts/store.ts). So the design's self-healing invariant is 'server restart wipes in-memory device map -> 401 -> re-register -> cursor reset -> idempotent full re-pull' — which works ONLY because auth is ephemeral. Phase 0's durable state / MUST-FIX (fix 'keyless any bearer passes') durablizes device auth so tokens survive restart, while the oplog+seqs remain in-memory (explicitly excluded from durable Supabase). Once that lands: restart -> token still valid -> no 401 -> no cursor reset; seqs reset to 0; post-restart rows minted at seq 1,2,3 are <= the client's held cursor N and are silently, permanently never delivered (server-oplog/API-platform inbound content). The double-deliver direction is harmless due to deterministic-ID idempotent ingest, but the skip direction is real silent data loss with no server-side detection. Not already mitigated: no maxSeq/stale-cursor check, no non-401 reset path. Multi-instance would amplify but the system runs ONE instance and horizontal scale isn't in the stated phases, so that half is speculative; the restart path is concrete/near-term. Severity medium (not high) because impact is scoped to server-oplog-delivered API-platform content (iMessage is local), and it is contingent on Phase 0 durablizing auth without also adding cursor invalidation — an implementation choice a competent Phase 0 could avoid. planStatus unaddressed: Phase 0 elaboration specifies only the systemCore/Safety/auth fix and says nothing about cursor invalidation or oplog seq monotonicity/migration.
refined: Because the client cursor resets only on a 401/re-registration and the server oplog seq is a per-process counter reset to 0 on restart with no maxSeq/stale-cursor guard, Phase 0's durable device auth removes the restart 401 (the sole reset trigger) while leaving the oplog ephemeral, so rows minted at low seqs after any restart are silently and permanently skipped for devices holding a higher pre-restart cursor.

title: Enrichment cache keyed on the unstable personID and never synced: identity re-clustering orphans it (paid re-fetch) and every device re-fetches independently
mechanism: PersonEnrichment is keyed solely by personID, is explicitly device-local (absent from syncTables, cascades with its person), and is considered fresh for 7 days (isStale). Two consequences. (1) When the identity graph re-mints a personID (the min-UUID split-brain above) or a manual merge tombstones the old person, enrichment(forPerson:) for the new/surviving id returns nil, forcing a fresh fan-out to the two PAID upstreams (Unipile + Exa), while the old row lingers as garbage. (2) Because it is device-local, each device independently fetches and independently ages its own 7-day window; there is no shared/server cache today. The plan's proposed server enrichment cache (Phase 5) would sit in front of a client cache that keys on a personID the server cannot reproduce (personID is a device-local identity-graph artifact, not derivable from a stable global key), so the two caches key on different identifiers and diverge / double-spend rather than dedup.
breaksAt: Any account with identity churn or >1 device; cost scales with paid Unipile+Exa calls (guarded only by a per-process 10/60s window, no cache).
reasoning: Core is real but the mechanism is partly overstated. VERIFIED: person_enrichment is absent from syncTables (OsmoStore+Sync.swift:7), so it is device-local; the server route (web/app/api/enrich/person/route.ts:9-23) and orchestrator (web/lib/enrich/person.ts) hold NO cache and fan out to paid Unipile+Exa on every call, guarded only by a per-process 10/60s window; each device ages its own 7-day isStale window (PersonEnrichment.swift:57-58). So multi-device accounts do double-fetch and there is no shared cache — consequence (2) holds. Merge orphaning also holds but via a DIFFERENT mechanism than claimed: mergePeople (OsmoStore.swift:450-454) SOFT-deletes the loser (SyncMeta deletedAt), so the FK onDelete:.cascade (OsmoDatabase.swift:232) never fires and the loser's enrichment row lingers forever; persons are hard-deleted only on full wipe (OsmoStore+Shell.swift:250), making the table's 'cascades away with its person' comment (PersonEnrichment.swift:6-7) effectively dead. REFUTED/OVERSTATED: 'identity re-cluster re-mints a personID' does not happen within a device — resolvePeople reuses the existing person whenever any member is already linked (OsmoStore.swift:410-424); the min-UUID DeterministicID.v5 (line 419) only runs at first mint, so personID is stable within a device and re-clustering does not orphan enrichment. The Phase-5 'server cache diverges/double-spends against a personID-keyed client cache' is speculative: the server never receives personID (wire req is {name,linkedinHandle,hints}, route.ts:33-45), so a natural request-keyed server cache would dedup cross-device and be immune to personID churn. Impact is bounded by the 7-day cache, 10/60s rate limit, and in-flight/empty-session dedup (AppModel.swift:1290-1292) — a linear per-device paid-cost multiplier plus negligible orphan rows, not runaway or a correctness break, hence low. Phase 5 ('enrichment cache') targets the no-shared-cache cost but not orphan cleanup or client key hygiene, so partial.
refined: Enrichment is device-local (not in syncTables) with no server cache, so every device independently pays Unipile+Exa per person on its own 7-day window, and merge tombstones (soft-deletes, so onDelete:.cascade never fires) leave orphaned enrichment rows — a bounded per-device cost/garbage issue; the claimed within-device personID re-mint is refuted by resolvePeople's reuse branch and the Phase-5 divergence is speculative since the server keys on the request, not personID.

title: The server-enforced free-tier bill guard lives in a different (ephemeral) store from the tier and is keyed on the volatile deviceId, so it resets to zero on every redeploy or re-register
mechanism: checkAndConsume reads/writes the weekly counter via store.usage/bumpUsage — the in-memory MemoryConnectionsStore.usage map — while the tier that decides 'unlimited' is read from the DURABLE accounts subscription (subscriptionForDevice). The counter is keyed per-deviceId. Because (a) the usage map is wiped on every restart and (b) the deviceId itself is re-minted on every 401 re-register (device-identity finding), a free user gets a fresh 15-draft allowance after any redeploy or by simply provoking re-registration — defeating the stated purpose ('the guard a lying client can't bypass'). The counter (ephemeral) and the tier (durable) also diverge in lifetime, so a device that was capped can be simultaneously 'over quota' per the durable tier's expectation yet counted at zero. Note: within a single Node process the read-modify-write itself is not racy — bumpUsage is synchronous with no await inside — so the real defect is cross-store durability + volatile keying, not concurrent double-spend.
breaksAt: Every free user, every deploy — Anthropic spend has no durable per-account ceiling backing it.
reasoning: Code confirms the mechanism. The free-tier counter lives ONLY in MemoryConnectionsStore.usage (memoryStore.ts:129,280-290), whose state is freshState() on globalThis.__osmoConnStore (memoryStore.ts:139,336-338); there is no Postgres/ConnectionsStore durable impl (only a 'Postgres later' comment, line 4), so it is wiped on every restart/redeploy. In suggest/route.ts:67-69 the tier that grants 'unlimited' is read from getAccounts().subscriptionForDevice — the Supabase-backed durable accounts store (store.ts:291-295,386-401) — while checkAndConsume writes the ephemeral counter, so the two genuinely diverge in durability. checkAndConsume/bumpUsage key on deviceId (quota.ts:32,36), and registerDevice() mints a fresh dev-${randomUUID()} with no dedup/linking each call (memoryStore.ts:160-168), with device/register/route.ts:2 documenting re-registration 'on any 401' — so a free user gets a fresh 15-draft allowance after any redeploy or by simply re-registering, defeating the code's own claim (suggest/route.ts:60) that this is 'the guard a lying client can't bypass.' grep confirms bumpUsage has exactly one call-site and no durable usage/quota table exists, so nothing mitigates it. The finding correctly disclaims intra-process double-spend (bumpUsage is synchronous) and scopes the defect to cross-store durability + volatile keying, which matches the code. Severity medium is appropriate: the guard is inert keyless (no bill to protect), the unbounded-spend amplification leans on the separate open-registration/deviceId-mint finding it cites, and durable state is an explicitly known/planned Phase-0 gap; standing alone the residual (reset-on-redeploy of ~15 drafts per device) is a real but bounded margin leak. planStatus partial: Phase 0 'Security & durable state' names durable state as a goal but its elaboration specifies only the systemCore/Safety/auth MUST-FIX, not durable/account-keyed quota metering.
refined: The server free-tier bill guard stores its weekly counter only in the ephemeral, deviceId-keyed MemoryConnectionsStore while reading the 'unlimited' tier from the durable accounts DB, so the counter resets to zero on every redeploy and can be reset at will by re-registering (which mints a new deviceId), leaving Anthropic spend with no durable per-account ceiling — contradicting the code's own 'guard a lying client can't bypass' claim.
